Options B-E are all directly contradicted by or unaddressed in the source (citrate is not universal; heparin is used in some procedures; TPE, not lipoprotein apheresis, is the citrate-anticoagulated procedure; no prophylactic-platelet-transfusion requirement is described anywhere). SRC-010's mention of HIT as a recognized entity provides reasonable premise support. No credible alternative answer exists among the options; well grounded and internally consistent with real-world apheresis practice (heparin/HELP-based LDL apheresis).

Core reasoning is well grounded: SRC-006 states platelets carry ABO, HLA class I (A,B), and HPA antigens; SRC-010 states HLA/HPA antibody assays are critical to ITP investigation; SRC-028 confirms HLA class II is restricted to APCs (correctly sinks option B); SRC-010's Donath-Landsteiner/anti-P/PCH fact correctly sinks option E. However, option D's rationale claims 'the sources classify Bernard-Soulier syndrome as a congenital platelet function (glycoprotein) disorder' — the word 'glycoprotein' is never used in any of the four sources (verified by grep); SRC-006 only says 'congenital (e.g., Bernard-Soulier, Glanzmann's)' with no mechanism detail. This is a minor invented/embellished attribution to 'the sources,' so source_support is downgraded to partial rather than pass. Also note option E (Donath-Landsteiner/PCH) is an easy give-away distractor since it's RBC- not platelet-directed, slightly weakening discrimination value, though this doesn't affect correctness of A as the single best answer.

Correct answer and core distractors are well grounded: SRC-006 explicitly states genotyping is essential for finding compatible donors 'when an antibody is weak or when HPA reagents are unavailable' — matching the stem's exact scenario; SRC-028 correctly defines virtual crossmatch as an HLA (not HPA) tool, sinking option C; SRC-010's TA-GVHD/irradiation fact correctly sinks option E. As in Q0, option D's rationale again asserts the sources describe Bernard-Soulier as a 'glycoprotein (function) disorder' — 'glycoprotein' is not present anywhere in the four sources, an embellished/unsupported attribution, so source_support is partial rather than pass. Secondary quality note: the blueprint node is 'General Tests to Investigate Thrombocytopenia,' but this question's clinical action pivots into platelet-donor selection/compatibility logistics rather than diagnostic testing per se — a mild topical drift, though the tested fact (genotyping indication) is itself squarely sourced.

All asserted facts are accurately and specifically grounded: SRC-006 (Node 65) defines CCI exactly as 'accounts for the patient's body surface area and the number of platelets transfused'; SRC-028 (Section 2, Alloimmune Refractoriness) states the appropriate consult is 'provision of HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelets for patients failing to achieve expected incremental increases' — a near-verbatim match to option A; SRC-010's TA-GVHD/gamma-irradiation fact correctly sinks option D. No invented or contradicted facts found. However, this question is materially about managing alloimmune platelet-transfusion refractoriness (a donor-selection/therapeutic-management scenario), not about 'General Tests to Investigate Thrombocytopenia' as the assigned blueprint node (S1.d.ii, C) specifies — the CCI/HLA-match content fits better under a platelet-transfusion-support or HLA-matching node than under the diagnostic-testing node for ITP. Flagging this placement mismatch at medium severity since the entire question stem and answer center on transfusion management rather than diagnostic work-up, despite being fully sourced.

SRC-002 Node 47 explicitly states 'Vitamin K Deficiency: Leads to the production of nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, and X (PIVKA)' and separately lists 'Urgent Warfarin Reversal' under the same node's Management Logic, but the source never explicitly states the molecular mechanism 'warfarin blocks vitamin K-dependent carboxylation' or explicitly equates warfarin's action with the 'Vitamin K Deficiency' bullet — that link is standard external medical knowledge (warfarin = VKA) rather than a sentence actually present in the source. The downstream effect (nonfunctional PIVKA factors II/VII/IX/X) is well supported; the causal/mechanistic phrasing is an inference beyond the literal text. Options B (liver disease sparing VIII/vWF), C (DIC consumption + fibrinolysis), and D (Factor VIII inhibitor via bypassing-agent context) are all accurately drawn from the source. No other option is defensible as the 'best' mechanism, so single-best-answer holds and the key is correct.

Both sources directly and unambiguously support the key: SRC-051 Node 39 states PCC is 'Treatment of choice for the urgent reversal of Vitamin K Antagonists (VKAs) like warfarin in bleeding patients,' and SRC-002 Node 47 states 'PCC is preferred over FFP due to its lower volume and faster normalization of the INR' — this is an almost verbatim match to the stem's framing ('most rapidly normalize the INR while minimizing the infused volume'). Distractors are each correctly grounded: recombinant FVIIa is tied only to Factor VIII inhibitor bypassing (not VKA reversal) per source; albumin's only described role is as TPE replacement fluid; fibrinogen concentrate is described only as a cryoprecipitate alternative for fibrinogen boosting. Minor note: the specific vignette details (ICH, INR 6.1) are plausible clinical dressing not verbatim in the source, but they do not introduce any unsupported factual claim being tested — the tested claim (PCC > FFP on volume/speed) is squarely sourced. Options D and E are very weak/easily-eliminated distractors (unrelated products), which slightly reduces discrimination but is not a correctness problem.

Core mechanism is solidly grounded: SRC-031 line 19 explicitly defines cryoprecipitate as 'the cold-insoluble portion of FFP; a concentrated source of Fibrinogen, Factor VIII, Factor XIII, and von Willebrand Factor,' which directly supports key A. The ADAMTS13/TTP distractor (B) is well supported by SRC-031 line 77 and SRC-023 line 79 (plasma/TPE replace ADAMTS13 to cleave ultra-large vWF multimers in TTP, a distinct disease from primary vWF deficiency). However, distractors C (INR) and D (volume expansion) are grounded only in statements the source makes about 'plasma'/'plasma products' generically (SRC-031 lines 61, 65), not cryoprecipitate specifically -- the option rationale text asserts 'the source specifically warns' about cryoprecipitate's INR/volume effects when the source's actual language is about plasma. This is a defensible mechanistic extension (cryo lacks factors II/VII/IX/X and is not used for volume expansion either) but is not literally stated for cryoprecipitate, so it's an inference beyond the text rather than a direct quote. Independently, A is clearly the single best answer -- no genuine ambiguity, just a minor sourcing looseness in two distractor rationales.

The keyed fact is explicitly and strongly grounded: SRC-002 line 35 states liver disease is 'characterized by decreased synthesis of nearly all clotting factors (except Factor VIII and vWF)' -- directly supporting answer A over B/C/D, since SRC-002 line 39 separately confirms Factors II, VII, IX, and X become nonfunctional/decreased in vitamin K deficiency (the vitamin-K-dependent factors named in distractors C and D), and fibrinogen/FXIII (option B) are reasonably read into the 'nearly all clotting factors' decrease. However, the exclusion of option E (Protein C and Protein S) is weakly sourced: Protein C is never mentioned anywhere in any of the three approved sources, and the cited support for Protein S (SRC-031's PF24 definition, about storage-related lability of Protein S in plasma products, not about hepatic synthetic function) is tangential and does not actually establish how Protein C/S behave in liver disease. This is an inferential leap dressed as sourced support. Additionally, the stem's phrase 'preserved (or even elevated)' asserts FVIII may be elevated in liver disease, a detail not stated in SRC-002 (which only says 'except,' i.e., not decreased) -- a minor unverified embellishment. Independently, A remains the only defensible answer on both source and general medical-knowledge grounds, so no genuine single-best-answer ambiguity exists, but the option-E rationale specifically overclaims source support it doesn't have.

None of the four approved sources mention vitamin K, vitamin K deficiency, or hemorrhagic disease of the newborn at all -- the blueprint node's core content is absent from the corpus. The distractor mechanisms (FNAIT platelet destruction, HDFN extravascular RBC destruction) are accurately sourced to SRC-017 Domain VII. But the keyed mechanism -- that vitamin K deficiency produces an isolated plasma coagulation-factor deficiency correctable by FFP -- is never stated in any source; it is justified only by repurposing SRC-032 Node 16's adult Massive Transfusion Protocol lab triggers (explicitly framed for '>=8-10 RBC units to an adult in <24 hours'), which is a contextual mismatch when applied to a term neonate's isolated coagulopathy. Medically the vignette is accurate and A is the only defensible answer given the normal platelet count, but the grounding for the central teaching point is not present in these four sources.

The lab-trigger logic (INR>1.5/aPTT>60 -> plasma; platelet <50,000 -> platelets; fibrinogen <100 -> cryo) is accurately and directly quoted from SRC-032 Node 16, and the IVIG/HDFN rationale is accurately sourced to SRC-017 Domain VII Management, so the arithmetic/threshold-matching in this item is well grounded. However, SRC-032's triggers are explicitly an adult massive-transfusion-protocol framework, not established anywhere in the sources as applicable to a 5-week-old infant with suspected late vitamin K deficiency bleeding, and no source discusses vitamin K deficiency or late HDN onset/pathophysiology at all. The keyed answer (FFP) is medically correct and the only defensible choice given the numbers, but the underlying clinical entity being tested is unsupported by this source set.

Core teaching point is well grounded: SRC-044 states verbatim 'HPA-1a: Primary target in Neonatal Alloimmune Thrombocytopenia (NAIT) and Post-Transfusion Purpura (PTP)' and its Transfusion Reactions classification table lists 'PTP (HPA-1a Ab)' under Delayed (>24h) Immune and 'TRALI (HLA/HNA Ab)' under Acute Immune, directly supporting option A and refuting D. SRC-006 confirms platelets carry only ABO, HLA Class I (A and B), and HPA (no Class II listed), supporting exclusion of B, and separately classifies Glanzmann/Bernard-Soulier as congenital/acquired platelet function disorders (not antibody-mediated), supporting exclusion of C. SRC-031 lists splenomegaly among nonimmune causes of poor platelet increment, supporting exclusion of E. However, several vignette specifics (gravida 3 para 3, exactly 'eight days' onset, platelet count '3,000/µL') are invented clinical-scenario detail not verifiable against any of the four sources -- they are consistent with real-world PTP teaching but are not themselves sourced facts, so the stem is not 100% fully grounded even though the tested inference and keyed answer are solid and singularly correct.

Correct answer is well grounded: SRC-006 states verbatim under Subdomain Node 62 'Genotyping Utility: Molecular testing (genotyping) is essential for finding compatible donors when an antibody is weak or when HPA reagents are unavailable,' which matches the stem's weak/inconsistent antisera reactions plus limited reagent supply, and clearly supports A over B (DAT is only discussed in the sources for RBC-bound antibody/AIHA workups), C (ABO is only one of three platelet antigen systems per SRC-006), and D (SRC-031 confirms apheresis reduces donor exposure vs. pooled 5-donor products). One overclaim: both the option D rationale and the SRC-031 source_reference assert apheresis platelets are 'easier to match when a patient requires antigen/antibody-directed selection' or 'preferred when matching is needed' generally -- but the actual SRC-031 text only says apheresis is 'easier to match for HLA if the patient becomes refractory,' never mentioning HPA-matching benefit specifically, so this is a mild generalization beyond what is stated. This does not change the correct answer or create a second defensible option, since A is independently and uniquely supported by the Genotyping Utility bullet.

Fully grounded in SRC-028: extreme polymorphism + linkage disequilibrium at the HLA locus; Class I (A,B,C) on nucleated cells/platelets vs Class II (DR,DQ,DP) restricted to APCs; Class III encodes complement/cytokines 'rather than classic histocompatibility antigens'; HNA/MICA explicitly listed as separate non-HLA determinants. All three distractors are directly contradicted by source text, leaving A as the unambiguous single best answer. Fits the blueprint node (polymorphism) well.

Core facts trace directly to SRC-003 (Bg = HLA class I weakly expressed on RBCs, removed by chloroquine treatment or EDTA/glycine; CH/RG destroyed by enzymes; KN reactive only by IAT and not clinically significant; JMH common >50 and not clinically significant). Option C correctly inverts the source's actual KN statement, making it a valid distractor. Key A is well supported and is the best answer among options. Two quality concerns: (1) the underlying Bg fact in the source is a single terse sentence, yet the question adds unsourced specificity ('chloroquine diphosphate,' 'EDTA/glycine acid') and builds an elaborate vignette on thin source material; (2) this question actually tests RBC-serology/Bg-distractor recognition, not 'polymorphism of the HLA system and nomenclature' as named by the assigned blueprint node - content is accurate but arguably filed under the wrong node.

Fully grounded in SRC-028: 'Consulting on the provision of HLA-matched or HLA-selected platelets for patients failing to achieve expected incremental increases' directly supports key A; the Virtual Crossmatch expert note (requires donor's documented HLA type) correctly refutes option D; HNA/MICA as separate non-HLA determinants correctly refutes option C. No invented or contradicted facts. However, this question tests clinical management of alloimmune platelet refractoriness, not 'polymorphism of the HLA system and nomenclature' as named by the assigned blueprint node - accurate HLA content but not a good fit for this specific sub-node, and neither this question nor the set as a whole tests HLA nomenclature at all.

Across all four approved sources, the ONLY sentence mentioning HLA is one line in SRC-043: 'The HLA System: MHC Class I/II biochemistry and typing for platelet refractoriness.' That line supports that HLA typing is used to work up refractoriness, but the actual asserted mechanism ('alloantibodies against MHC I/II on transfused platelets causing immune clearance') is never stated in the source — it is a reasonable outside-knowledge inference, not a sourced fact, so I cannot rate this a full pass. Distractors C (febrile/cytokines), D (TA-GVHD), and E (bacterial sepsis) are genuinely supported by SRC-043/SRC-013 language. Distractor B (isohemagglutinins vs RBCs) is a very weak/off-topic distractor requiring no source knowledge to eliminate. Medically the keyed answer A is correct and the single best answer (independent check = A). Bigger issue: the blueprint node is specifically 'Serologic Methods' for identifying HLA antigens/antibodies/alleles (e.g., CDC/NIH lymphocytotoxicity, flow crossmatch, solid-phase/Luminex assays), and this item tests none of that — it tests clinical recognition of immune platelet refractoriness, not a serologic identification method. That is a real blueprint-node mismatch.

This item describes a panel-reactive-antibody (PRA)-style workup (serum tested against an HLA-typed donor cell panel, broad reactivity against shared common specificities) and asks for its interpretation. None of the four sources mention PRA, panel testing, percent-panel-reactive, lymphocytotoxicity, or any comparable serologic method — the entire premise and the correct-answer interpretation are unsourced extrapolations from the single generic 'typing for platelet refractoriness' line in SRC-043. This is the item most conceptually adjacent to the 'Serologic Methods' blueprint node (PRA/panel testing is a classic serologic method), yet it is also the least grounded in the actual provided sources — essentially invented content dressed as source-grounded. Distractors B (ABO), C (bacterial culture), D (TA-GVHD/irradiation), and E are each tied to source facts about what those other tests/measures are for, so the distractor logic is fine, but they are easy eliminations that don't require sourced HLA knowledge (weak distractors). Medically, A is the correct and only defensible interpretation of broad PRA-panel reactivity (independent answer = A, agrees with key), but source support for the tested fact pattern itself is essentially absent.

The keyed management strategy (HLA-matched/antigen-avoiding donor selection) is a reasonable real-world extension of SRC-043's single line 'typing for platelet refractoriness,' but the source never explicitly states that HLA-matched platelet selection is the corrective strategy — this is inferred, not sourced. The exclusions of irradiation (TA-GVHD prevention) and leukoreduction (FNHTR/CMV prevention) are well supported verbatim by SRC-043's 'Pretransfusion Processing' bullet, and the prophylactic (10k) vs procedural (50k-100k) trigger values in option E match SRC-043's 'Platelet Hemotherapy' bullet exactly. Medically A is correct and the single best answer (independent check = A, agrees). Two structural concerns: (1) this is the third vignette in the set built almost entirely off the same one-sentence HLA fact, testing essentially the same underlying teaching point as items 0 and 1 (mechanism → interpretation → management), which is heavy duplicate coverage of a very thin source base; (2) like the other two items, this tests clinical management of HLA alloimmunization rather than the specified blueprint node 'Serologic Methods' for identifying HLA antigens/antibodies/alleles, so none of the three items actually hits the target node.

SRC-010 (blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-board-curriculum.md, 'II. Domain: Cell and Tissue Therapy') states verbatim: 'FDA 21 CFR Part 1271 regulates tissue banking to prevent disease transmission' and 'Tissue service activities include bidirectional traceability between donor and recipient,' with HCT/Ps defined in the same subdomain block. This directly grounds option A. SRC-043 line 53 ('Pretransfusion Processing: Irradiation (TA-GVHD prevention) and leukoreduction (FNHTR/CMV prevention)') confirms B and C describe blood-component measures, not a stated tissue-banking rationale, so they are correctly excluded. SRC-028 (domain-4, Section 7) confirms Form FDA 3486/BPD criteria exactly as described, but nowhere frames BPD reporting as 'the primary reason the regulation exists,' so D is correctly a distractor. No invented facts found; A is the only defensible answer.

The core capability ('bidirectional traceability between donor and recipient') is stated verbatim in SRC-010. However, the specific elaboration in option A — 'trace... forward again to identify any other recipients of tissue recovered from that same donor' — is not itself spelled out in the one-line source sentence; it is a reasonable but unstated extension of 'bidirectional,' so I rate this partial rather than a full pass. Distractors are cleanly eliminated: SRC-043 line 49 confirms immediate-spin/AHG crossmatch is described only for RBC pretransfusion testing (not bone allografts, option B); SRC-043 lines 153/155 tie mandatory FDA fatality reporting to death, not a nonfatal infection (option C); SRC-044 line 21 confirms Hardy-Weinberg is used only for phenotype-frequency calculation, an essentially irrelevant/weak distractor for a traceability question (option D). A remains the only defensible answer despite the minor extrapolation.

Each individual building block is present in the sources: SRC-010 states bidirectional donor-recipient traceability; SRC-028 (Section 7) states BPD/Form FDA 3486 criteria (manufacturing deviation, distributed product, safety/purity/potency impact); SRC-043/domain-4 state nonconforming-event detection/documentation/correction is a facility (not FDA-initiated) responsibility. However, the sources never explicitly combine these into a stated tissue-specific workflow, and the BPD/Form 3486 section is written in a generic 'Regulatory and Accreditation Oversight' context without an explicit statement that it applies to tissue allografts (as opposed to blood components) — this application to HCT/Ps is inferred via the general fact that FDA/CBER also regulates tissue under 1271, not directly asserted. The term 'quarantine' and the specific action of 'notify the partner facilities' also do not appear verbatim in any of the four sources; they are constructed by combining the traceability and CAPA/nonconforming-event principles. This is a defensible synthesis question (options B, C, D are each clearly contradicted or unsupported — B claims processing eliminates risk, which no source states; C contradicts the facility's own detect/document/correct duty; D wrongly limits reporting to fatalities only), so A remains the single best answer, but the correct-answer rationale presents an inferred combined policy as though it were directly stated, which is worth flagging.

Core tested fact is explicitly and directly grounded: SRC-010 line 27 states verbatim that MHC Class I (HLA-A,-B,-C) is on nearly all nucleated cells while Class II (HLA-DR,-DQ,-DP) is restricted to antigen-presenting cells — this is exactly the mechanism needed to explain B-cell-only reactivity, and it is a textbook-accurate, unambiguous fact (T cells are Class I+/Class II-, B cells are Class I+/Class II+). Answer B is clearly the single best answer. Minor issue: the distractor rationales for C (HPA) and D (ABO) assert that these systems are 'distinct from' or 'not a discriminator' of lymphocyte reactivity — true immunology but not explicitly stated in SRC-006's node 62 text, which only lists ABO/HLA-I/HPA as platelet antigens without discussing lymphocyte expression. Low-risk inference rather than an invented or contradicted fact, so graded partial rather than fail.

The two underlying facts are each explicitly sourced (SRC-010 line 27: HLA Class I on nearly all nucleated cells including T cells; SRC-006 node 62 line 27: platelets carry ABO, HLA Class I, and HPA). However, the specific diagnostic algorithm in the stem — retest platelet-reactive serum against isolated T lymphocytes to distinguish anti-HLA Class I from anti-HPA — is a constructed synthesis of those two facts rather than something either source states directly; no source describes this testing protocol. It is a medically valid and defensible extension, so not failed, but it goes beyond a direct restatement. Also, option C introduces a B-lymphocyte reactivity result even though the stem's retest is described as being performed only against isolated T lymphocytes, so that distractor describes data the described protocol never generates (minor construction flaw, not fatal). Independently, A is the correct answer: shared platelet/T-cell reactivity is the only option consistent with an antigen common to both cell types (HLA Class I), since HPA is platelet-restricted per SRC-006.

The diagnostic portion (shared platelet/T-lymphocyte reactivity = anti-HLA Class I, per SRC-010 line 27) and the CCI definition (SRC-006 node 65) are properly grounded, and the distractor eliminations for B/D/E are reasonably grounded by contrast (irradiation ties to TA-GVHD per SRC-010 line 75/SRC-019 line 67; splenectomy/IVIG tie to WAIHA per SRC-019 line 53 — none of these sources describe them as platelet-refractoriness treatments, so ruling them out via absence-of-evidence is fair). But the pivotal claim that makes A correct — that HLA-matched or crossmatch-compatible platelet donors are the appropriate management step for HLA-alloimmune platelet transfusion refractoriness — is not stated anywhere in the four sources. The cited support (SRC-010 Domain VIII, line 109: 'HLA matching requirements' as a major concept in HPC Transplantation) concerns donor selection for hematopoietic stem cell transplantation/engraftment, a materially different clinical context from selecting platelet products to avoid a recipient's anti-HLA antibody. The explanation itself concedes this is merely 'analogous to the HLA-matching concepts described for hematopoietic transplantation' rather than a stated fact — i.e. real outside clinical knowledge (medically correct) is being extrapolated, not something established by the approved sources. Given the project's source-grounded design premise, this is a genuine, high-severity grounding failure on the single most load-bearing claim in the question (the correct answer itself). Secondary concern: the question's core content (a transfusion-management decision) sits outside the blueprint node's actual scope of 'Cellular Methods' for HLA antigen/antibody/allele identification; it uses a cellular assay only as a stem device before pivoting to an unrelated management topic. Medically/independently, A is still the only defensible answer among the options, hence single_best_answer=true and answer_agrees=true — the issue is grounding, not multiplicity of correct options.

The keyed rationale (a single serologic HLA antigen can map to multiple alleles, requiring nucleic acid/PCR methods to resolve) is real HLA science but is NOT established anywhere in the 4 approved sources for HLA. The cited support (SRC-044 lines 9-11, 'DNA Analysis: PCR-RFLP, SSP, Bead-chip') is explicitly scoped to 'red cell genotyping and resolving complex phenotypes' in a red-cell/blood-group-genetics subsection, not to HLA. SRC-044's actual HLA section (lines 69-73) lists HLA 'Testing' as only Lymphocytotoxicity, Flow Cytometry, and Virtual Crossmatching -- no DNA/PCR method is listed there at all, which arguably undercuts rather than supports the premise. SRC-010 and SRC-002/SRC-013 add nothing further on HLA molecular typing. Distractors B, C, D are individually well-refuted by the sources (Class I/II distribution per SRC-010 line 27; validity of lymphocytotoxicity/flow cytometry per SRC-044 lines 69-73; ABO vs. HLA-matching distinction per SRC-010 line 109), and A remains the only medically defensible answer among the four, so I agree with the key -- but the question's central fact is grounded in outside domain knowledge, not in these approved sources, despite being framed and cited as source-grounded.

Same core defect as question 0: the premise that nucleic acid-based typing has higher resolution than antigen-level serologic typing (and can reveal an allele-level mismatch within one serologic HLA-B group) is accurate HLA science but is not stated in any of the 4 sources; the cited SRC-044 lines 9-11 passage is about red cell genotyping, not HLA, and SRC-044's HLA-specific 'Testing' list (lines 69-73) omits nucleic acid methods entirely. SRC-010 line 27 correctly supports rejecting option C (Class I is on nearly all nucleated cells, not restricted to APCs), and general logic correctly eliminates B and D, so A is still the only defensible answer and matches the key. Additionally flagging duplicate-coverage: this question and question 0 test the identical fact pattern (antigen-level match can mask allele-level mismatch; nucleic acid typing has higher resolution) from two clinical framings (unrelated HPC donor selection vs. cord blood unit release), which is substantial conceptual overlap for a 2-question set on one blueprint node.

SRC-020's Position Effect definition ('Reduced expression of D when the C antigen is inherited in trans to the D gene (e.g., Dce/dCe)') is quoted almost verbatim and uniquely identifies A given the genotype clue (Dce/dCe, C in trans to RHD) — no other option's mechanism matches this specific genetic finding. RHD deletion, Partial D, Del, and Blocked Rh are each accurately defined per SRC-020 and correctly excluded (deletion would give a fully negative, not 1+, reaction; Partial D is epitope loss with alloanti-D risk, not a trans-position dosage effect; Del is undetectable even at AHG, unlike this 1+ IS reaction; Blocked Rh applies to antibody-coated fetal cells in HDFN, not this non-transfused adult). Gap: the specific reactivity magnitudes in the stem (1+ at IS vs. a typical 3-4+) and the claim that position effect reactivity is detectable at immediate spin are not stated anywhere in SRC-020 — plausible vignette embellishment consistent with general Rh serology but not verified against the provided source text. This does not change the correctness of the keyed answer.

The weak D serologic pattern in the stem (IS nonreactive, AHG 2+) matches SRC-020 verbatim ('Weak D Serology: Initial anti-D testing is negative or ≤2+, but becomes reactive at the AHG phase'), and 'Weak D Testing: Mandatory for all donor units; optional for transfusion recipients' directly supports why AHG-phase testing was performed and why it cannot be skipped (refuting A). However, the specific rule invoked to justify Rh-positive labeling — that any donor demonstrating D reactivity must be labeled Rh-positive — is explicitly stated in SRC-020 only under 'Partial D Donors: Must be classified as Rh-positive... even if they type weakly,' not verbatim for a weak-D-pattern donor without genotype/partial-D confirmation as depicted here. The extrapolation is standard, uncontroversial blood banking practice and doesn't threaten the answer's correctness, but it is not a literal one-to-one source match. Distractors C (discard unit), D (restricted issue pending review), and E (repeat with different lot) are correctly and clearly excluded — none of these alternative dispositions appear in the source, and the IS-negative/AHG-positive pattern is explicitly the expected weak D presentation, not an error state.

Fully and precisely grounded in SRC-020. The 'Blocked Rh' definition ('fetal RBCs so heavily coated with maternal anti-D that they yield a false-negative Rh typing result') and the 'Blocked Rh Distractor' board-trap note ('If an infant's cells type as D-negative but have a 4+ DAT, perform an eluate to identify the blocked D antigen') map almost verbatim onto the stem and keyed answer B. Distractor rationales are each explicitly supported: DAT with anti-IgG (not anti-C3d) is stated as 'the most important serologic test for diagnosis' of HDFN (refutes C); KB test is explicitly for calculating maternal RhIg dose via FMH quantification, not for confirming infant Rh type (refutes D); and the passive-vs-active anti-D titer distinction (titer >4 indicates active alloimmunization) is a real but separate consideration that does not resolve the immediate typing problem (refutes E). No unsupported facts or ambiguity found.

Core mechanism (sensitization vs. agglutination; IgG too small to bridge gap in saline; AHG bridges the gap) is directly supported by SRC-032 Node 20 (lines 177-181, essentials-of-transfusion-medicine file). Distractor C's zeta-potential/albumin conflation and D's complement-lysis claim are correctly identified as unsupported/contradicted. Minor gap: option A's specific phrase 'AHG reagent bound the Fc portions of the cell-bound IgG' is a standard elaboration not verbatim in the source text (source only says AHG 'bind[s] to human globulins... attached to RBC antigens'), so this detail is inferred rather than directly quoted, though it does not change correctness. No other option is defensible; A is clearly the single best answer.

Well grounded: 'Check Cell Necessity' (line 215) and 'False Negatives: Inadequate washing (leading to neutralization of AHG)' (line 203) directly support option B's reasoning, and the PEG-reading-omission distractor (line 213) is accurately used to build and correctly reject option E. Option C is directly contradicted by the source statement that check cells must be added to 'all negative AHG tests.' Minor gap: the directive that 'the entire test must be repeated correctly' is a reasonable standard-practice inference rather than an explicit source statement, but it does not create ambiguity. B is clearly the single best answer; no other option is defensible.

The AHG-vs-immediate-spin crossmatch distinction is directly sourced (SRC-043: 'Immediate-spin (ABO only) vs. AHG crossmatch (alloantibody)'), and clinically significant antibody screening/ID is sourced (SRC-024 Diagnostic Approach). However, the specific procedural claim in option B/explanation that donor units must be selected as 'negative for the corresponding antigen' for an already-identified alloantibody is not explicitly stated in any of the four sources for this scenario — the only antigen-matching statement found (SRC-024, Prevention/Alloimmunization Mitigation) describes prophylactic C/E/K matching to reduce future alloimmunization in chronically transfused SCD patients, not the standard rule of avoiding antigen-positive units once a specific antibody is already present. The question's own metadata already flags this citation as 'moderate' confidence, which is appropriate. Despite this grounding gap, B is still clearly the best answer among the five options — A, C, D, and E are each directly contradicted by sourced facts (AHG-only detection of alloantibodies; antibody screen antibodies being clinically significant; non-emergency scheduled transfusion; need for AHG phase to detect IgG) — so single_best_answer holds even though full source support for the antigen-negative-selection component is not demonstrated.

SRC-024 defines dosage almost verbatim as used in the key: 'antibody reacts more strongly with RBCs from homozygous individuals than heterozygous individuals (common in Rh, Duffy, Kidd, and MNS systems).' SRC-001's 'Dosage-Only Reactivity' board-trap note (Kidd/Duffy weak/rule-out on heterozygous cell) matches the exact scenario. Distractor B (enzyme destruction) is correctly refuted — SRC-001 states ficin/papain ENHANCE Kidd antigens. Distractor D (DTT destruction) is correctly refuted — SRC-001 states DTT inactivates Kell and disulfide-bonded systems (Lutheran, Knops, Dombrock), not Kidd. Distractor E (low-prevalence antigen) is correctly distinguished from the 'unexplained incompatibility' scenario in SRC-001, which is a different clinical presentation. Distractor C is precluded by the stem's own premise that other specificities were already excluded. Independently the best answer is clearly A; fully grounded, no fabricated or contradicted facts.

Core answer is well supported: SRC-001 defines 'Selected Cell Panels: Test specific cells to differentiate remaining candidates or rule out antibodies masked by a known specificity,' which directly matches the K/Fya co-segregation scenario, and the '3 and 3 rule' confidence-interval concept is accurately cited to justify why premature reporting (option E) is inappropriate. Elution (option C) is correctly distinguished as applying to antibody already bound to RBCs, not serum-vs-reagent-cell panel testing. However, the rationale rejecting option D (Lewis-substance neutralization for a Fya/K distinction) rests on an absence-based inference — SRC-001 lists neutralization sources only for Lewis, P1, and Ch/Rg without an explicit statement that Duffy is unaffected by neutralization. That inference is reasonable but not verbatim-sourced, so I mark this partial rather than a full pass. The correct answer (B) itself is unambiguous and is the independently best choice.

The core mechanism is stated almost verbatim in SRC-001's 'Common Board Traps' section: 'Anti-CD38 Interference: This biologic therapy (Daratumumab) causes panreactivity in the IAT; it is mitigated by DTT treatment of reagent cells, which incidentally destroys Kell antigens.' This strongly supports option B being correct and clearly refutes option A (DTT panel cannot exclude anti-K). Option C is correctly distinguished from SRC-001's emergency-release provision (only for incomplete ID during an emergency, not routine ongoing practice). Option D's rouleaux/saline-replacement refutation is reasonably supported by SRC-001's rouleaux-vs-agglutination pearl combined with the fact that DTT (not saline) abolished the reactivity. Option E is correctly weighed against the 3-month-untransfused caveat for autoadsorption, though the source doesn't explicitly address why autoadsorption fails specifically for anti-CD38 interference. The one gap: the source states the DTT/Kell-destruction mechanism but does not explicitly recommend 'therefore select K-negative units' for this scenario — that specific clinical action is a reasonable and standard synthesis of the stated mechanism plus the general antigen-negative-unit-selection principle, but is not a verbatim-quoted recommendation in the provided sources for this exact scenario, so I mark source_support partial rather than a full pass. B remains the independently best and only defensible answer among the choices given.

Core mechanism is well grounded: SRC-044 line 49 explicitly ties Kidd (Jk) antibodies to 'rapid disappearance' and DHTR, line 137 explicitly labels DHTR as 'Anamnestic Jk/Kidd', and line 15 differentiates primary/secondary kinetics and IgG vs IgM significance. Distractor D is correctly refuted by Method 3-13 (enzymes ENHANCE Kidd detection, they destroy Duffy/MNS, not Kidd). However, option A's rationale asserts as sourced fact that 'anti-Jka is IgG, not IgM' — no source line explicitly classifies Jka antibody immunoglobulin class; this is a reasonable clinical inference from the DHTR/anamnestic/enzyme-enhancement entries but is not verbatim stated, so treat that specific sub-claim as inferred rather than directly sourced. This does not change the correct key. Only SRC-044 supports this question; SRC-028, SRC-031, and SRC-041 contain no Kidd/DHTR/anamnestic content at all.

Well grounded: SRC-044 line 89 ('Method 3-7, Saline Replacement, Distinguish true agglutination from rouleaux in multiple myeloma') and SRC-041 line 65 ('Category III (Protein): Rouleaux ... resolve by saline replacement') directly support the stem and key. Distractors are correctly excluded via SRC-044's other method entries (prewarm=cold antibodies line 87; enzyme=Kidd/Rh enhancement line 91; DTT/AET=Kell/CD38 line 93; cold-acid elution=warm autoantibody recovery, not listed near rouleaux). Minor issue: the stem/explanation's vivid morphological detail ('stack of coins'/'linear stacks rather than irregular clumps') is standard rouleaux terminology but is not verbatim present in either cited source — a stylistic embellishment, not a substantive factual error, and does not affect the correct answer or distractor logic.

Directly and unambiguously grounded: SRC-044 line 93 ('Method 3-18, DTT/AET Treatment, Destroy Kell antigens or denature CD38 for patients on Daratumumab') exactly supports the stem and key. Distractors are correctly excluded per the source's distinct method entries: two-stage enzyme=Kidd/Rh enhancement + Duffy/MNS destruction (line 91), acid glycine-EDTA=general antibody removal/Kell destruction (line 85), not tied to daratumumab in source. Option D (continue unmodified) and E (defer testing) are reasonably refuted as being inconsistent with the source's dedicated resolving technique. No unsupported or contradicted facts identified; only SRC-044 carries relevant content (SRC-028, SRC-031, SRC-041 have no daratumumab/CD38/DTT content), which is appropriately reflected in the sparse source_references list.

Core fact chain (IS crossmatch = ABO/IgM only; AHG crossmatch required for IgG alloantibodies like anti-D) is well supported by SRC-043 ('Pretransfusion Testing: Immediate-spin (ABO only) vs. AHG crossmatch (alloantibody)') and by SRC-020's statement that Weak D 'requires an indirect antiglobulin test (IAT) for detection' and that 'Rh antibodies (primarily IgG)...'. However, the specific mechanistic phrase in option A/explanation -- 'IgG-coated red cells do not directly agglutinate in saline; AHG reagent bridges sensitized cells to produce visible agglutination' -- is standard immunohematology fact but is not explicitly stated anywhere in these four sources; it is a reasonable synthesis/elaboration beyond the literal source text, so I rate support 'partial' rather than 'pass'. Distractors B (rouleaux), C (bacterial contamination), D (donor weak D testing), E (HLA/platelet refractoriness) are each correctly falsified against the sources (SRC-013 confirms bacterial testing is platelet-specific; SRC-020 confirms weak D testing is a separate mandatory donor-only process; master guide confirms HLA typing is tied to platelet refractoriness, not RBC AHG crossmatch). Option A is clearly the single best/only defensible answer.

SRC-043 states 'Clerical error is the #1 cause' of 'Acute Hemolytic Reactions (AHTR)' -- this is stated in the context of post-transfusion adverse-reaction causation, not explicitly as an explanation for a pre-transfusion immediate-spin crossmatch incompatibility discovered on specimen recheck. The question/explanation extends this fact to a slightly different (though closely related and clinically standard 'wrong blood in tube') scenario; this is a reasonable and conventional board-exam inference but is not verbatim/explicit in the source, so support is 'partial' rather than full 'pass'. The IS-detects-ABO-only logic itself is directly supported (SRC-043 line 49). Distractor rationales are well grounded: B correctly notes IgG antibodies (anti-D) require AHG and would not cause IS agglutination (SRC-020); C correctly notes DHTR is an anamnestic response occurring after a unit has already been transfused, not a pretransfusion IS finding (SRC-043 'Delayed Reactions: ...anamnestic'); D correctly separates donor weak-D testing from recipient ABO mismatch (SRC-020); E correctly notes TRALI is a post-transfusion pulmonary reaction, not a tube-agglutination finding (SRC-043 TRALI vs TACO). Option A remains clearly the single best answer given the stem's explicit clue (recollected specimen no longer matches the original ABO type).

Well grounded. SRC-020 explicitly states 'D-negative recipients should receive D-negative red cells' (Unit Selection) and 'Partial D Donors: Must be classified as Rh-positive to prevent sensitizing recipients, even if they type weakly,' directly falsifying option E. SRC-020's RhIg Protocol section (28 weeks' gestation, within 72 hours of delivery of a D-positive infant, post D-positive platelet transfusion) contains no indication for treating a patient who already has anti-D, correctly falsifying option C. SRC-043's IS-vs-AHG distinction supports the crossmatch method choice, and SRC-020's statement that 'Rh antibodies (primarily IgG) typically cause extravascular hemolysis' supports the rationale against giving D-positive units (option D). Option B (IS-only) is trivially wrong given the patient has a known clinically significant IgG alloantibody. Option A is the clear, singularly defensible answer.

Option A is correct BB/TM knowledge (validated BECS-based electronic crossmatch works by comparing already-confirmed ABO/Rh records rather than physically mixing serum and cells), and the three distractors are correctly refuted using solid, directly-quoted source definitions (SRC-032 Node 20: IAT is an in-vitro reagent-cell/serum reaction, not something a computer 'simulates'; PEG is a physical potentiator; polyspecific AHG detects C3d in a physical test). However, the core mechanistic claim in the key/explanation is not actually drawn from any RBC-specific electronic-crossmatch passage in these four sources -- none of the four documents defines or describes an RBC electronic/computer crossmatch mechanism at all. The cited support (SRC-028's 'Virtual Crossmatch' Expert Note) is explicitly about comparing a recipient's HLA ANTIBODY profile to a donor's HLA TYPE for platelet-refractoriness/transplant matching, not about ABO/Rh RBC compatibility logic. The item's own 'confidence: moderate' and overall 'support_confidence: low' labels correctly signal this is an analogical extrapolation rather than a directly sourced fact, but the explanation and stem present it as if it were the sourced description of the RBC process. This is a real, if modest, over-claiming of source grounding for the pivotal fact of the item.

Well grounded: SRC-003 Subdomain Node 29 states verbatim that 'If no historical record exists, many facilities require a second sample collected at a different time to verify the ABO group before issuing non-Group O RBCs,' which directly supports key A and correctly refutes distractor B (a single current type + negative screen is explicitly framed as insufficient absent history). The Group O rationale for option C is also directly and correctly sourced ('If an ABO discrepancy cannot be resolved before an urgent transfusion is needed, only Group O RBCs should be issued' -- this scenario has no discrepancy and is elective, so C is correctly a distractor). Option D (PEG) is a reasonable, if slightly generic, distractor grounded in the PEG definition. Minor concern: the question is really testing a general ABO-confirmation/pretransfusion-testing rule (Node 29) rather than an electronic-crossmatch-specific mechanic; the tie to 'computer-based release' in the stem is a plausible framing device but the tested fact itself is not specific to the electronic-crossmatch node -- a mild blueprint-scope stretch, not a factual problem.

Directly and clearly grounded: SRC-003 Node 27 states verbatim 'Facilities must maintain back-up paper documents and procedures for use when computerized information systems are unavailable,' which is an exact match for key A. Option C is correctly refuted using the sourced Group O rule (reserved for unresolved ABO discrepancy with urgent need -- not applicable to a stable patient with an established historical type during a downtime event). Option B's refutation ('back-up procedures exist to prevent exactly this delay') and option D's refutation (electronic release cannot proceed without the operative computer system) are reasonable inferences consistent with the sourced downtime/back-up-procedure requirement, though the source does not explicitly spell out that electronic release must stop entirely during an outage -- a minor inferential leap, but a low-risk and clinically standard one. No equally defensible alternative answer exists among the options.

SRC-003 (Node 29) directly defines WBIT as blood in a pretransfusion specimen not belonging to the labeled recipient and states most fatal ABO-incompatible transfusions arise from clerical/identification error rather than testing failure - matches the stem's exclusion of technical/reagent/interpretation error and the clean A-to-O discordance on repeat draw. SRC-012 (Node 51) confirms chimerism characteristically causes mixed-field agglutination (not a clean single-population type flip), correctly grounding rejection of option C. Option E (Bg/HLA Class I antigen misread as anti-A) is a very weak distractor - SRC-012 (Node 54) only describes Bg as a nuisance finding stripped by chloroquine in antibody workups, not a plausible mechanism for a monoclonal anti-A forward-typing false-positive; it is easily eliminated on scientific grounds alone, slightly reducing its discriminatory value, though this does not affect correctness of the key.

SRC-003 (Node 29) states verbatim that when no historical ABO record exists, many facilities require a second sample collected at a different time to verify the ABO group before issuing non-group O RBCs - exact match to the stem and key. The same node's bedside-labeling and two-identifier requirements support rejecting option C (relabeling a tube is never the accepted remedy; recollection is). Option E's claim that group AB is compatible with any recipient is factually backwards for RBCs and is correctly contrasted against the source's group-O guidance. No contradicted or invented facts found; scenario is non-urgent so recommending confirmatory recollection (rather than emergency O release) is correct and source-grounded.

SRC-012 (Node 55) defines Emergency Release precisely as urgent issuance of uncrossmatched group O RBCs requiring a signed physician's statement and clear labeling that testing is incomplete - matches option A word-for-word in substance. SRC-003 (Node 29 board trap) independently corroborates that group O (not AB) is issued when ABO status can't be resolved before an urgent transfusion, supporting rejection of option D. Options B/E (withholding transfusion) and C (skipping the physician statement / using unconfirmed group-specific blood) are all directly contradicted by the Emergency Release definition. Well-grounded, unambiguous.

Key A is correct and clearly the best answer among the five options. However, source grounding is thin: SRC-043's bullets are terse topic headers ('ABO System (001): Biochemistry of H, A, and B transferases'; 'Basic Immunology: Complement pathways and IgG vs. IgM clinical kinetics'; 'Acute Hemolytic Reactions (AHTR): Clerical error is the #1 cause; hallmark is visual hemolysis'). None of these bullets explicitly states that Group O cells lack A/B antigens, that AHTR is IgM/complement-mediated, or that the hallmark 'visual hemolysis' equals 'intravascular hemolysis.' These are true, standard blood-bank facts but are stitched together/extrapolated rather than directly quoted or stated in the approved sources, so the explanation and several option rationales (esp. for A and B) exceed what is literally grounded. The question's own self-declared 'support_confidence: moderate' is consistent with this gap.

Core fact is a near-verbatim match to SRC-043: 'Pretransfusion Testing: Immediate-spin (ABO only) vs. AHG crossmatch (alloantibody).' Key A is correct and the distractors (B alloantibody detection, C Rh compatibility, D full AHG equivalence, E delayed hemolytic reaction risk) are all clearly and correctly excluded by that same distinction — no invented facts found. However, the assigned blueprint node is specifically 'Emergency Release' (node S3.d.ii.4.g), and this stem never invokes emergency release (no unknown/uncrossmatched Group O issuance, no physician statement, no incomplete-testing labeling) — it is a general immediate-spin-vs-AHG crossmatch question, which is a different Domain 2 pretransfusion-testing concept adjacent to, but not the same as, emergency release. This is a blueprint-topic mismatch even though the content itself is accurate.

Strong, precise match to SRC-012 Node 55: 'Emergency Release: Standard for the urgent issuance of uncrossmatched Group O RBCs, requiring a signed physician's statement and clear labeling indicating testing is incomplete.' Option D's HBOC rationale matches SRC-012 Node 54 verbatim ('Currently available primarily through clinical trials or expanded access (compassionate use)'). Option E's MTP characterization matches SRC-043 ('Defined 1:1:1 ratios to combat trauma-induced coagulopathy'). All distractors are correctly and clearly excluded; this question directly and accurately targets the assigned Emergency Release blueprint node with no invented or unsupported facts.

Core content is well grounded: SRC-058 Node 31 explicitly states AHTR pathophysiology as 'IgM or IgG antibodies fix complement, leading to intravascular hemolysis, cytokine release, DIC, and potential acute renal failure' and etiology as 'most commonly caused by ABO incompatibility resulting from clerical or patient identification errors' (lines 21-23). SRC-003 Node 29 confirms the 'final check'/last-line-of-defense framing and that most fatal ABO-incompatible transfusions stem from clerical/ID errors (lines 133-135). Distractor rationales for B (DHTR, 2-14 days, Kidd/Rh), C (TRALI as inflammatory/noncardiogenic per SRC-043 line 81), D (anaphylaxis/IgA deficiency per SRC-058 line 77), and E (FNHTR as diagnosis of exclusion per SRC-058 line 75) are all accurately grounded. However, the stem's specific symptom cluster 'chills' and 'flank pain' is NOT found anywhere in the approved sources' AHTR content (SRC-058 Node 31 lists only hemoglobinemia/hemoglobinuria as defined findings, no symptom list with chills/flank pain); that exact triad (fever, chills, flank/back pain) appears verbatim only in a sibling, non-approved source file (noninfectious-complications-and-hazards-of-blood-transfusion.md) not in the approved set for this review. This is standard, uncontroversial clinical knowledge and does not affect the correctness of the keyed answer, but strictly it is stem content beyond the four approved sources. Independently verified A as the best answer; no viable competing option.

WBIT definition ('blood in a pretransfusion sample tube does not belong to the recipient identified on the label') and the bedside-labeling requirement are directly supported by SRC-003 Node 29 (lines 141, 149). AHTR workup steps (visual inspection for hemoglobinemia, DAT/mixed-field, repeat ABO of patient and unit) are directly supported by SRC-058 Node 31 (lines 39-43). DHTR timing/mechanism (distractor D) and FNHTR-as-exclusion (distractor E) are correctly grounded. The antisera Day-of-Use QC fact used to weigh against distractor C is correctly grounded in SRC-043's QC table (line 201). Two residual concerns: (1) as in Q0, the stem's 'low back pain' symptom is not explicit in the approved sources' AHTR content (only found in a non-approved sibling file), a minor stem-flavor gap. (2) Option C's wording ('ABO type correctly determined on both occasions... technical error produced the discrepant post-reaction result') is internally ambiguous/confusingly worded - a sharper distractor would have more cleanly proposed a reagent failure on the original specimen as an alternative to WBIT, which would have created genuine competition with A. As written, C implicitly denies a true ABO mismatch ever existed, which cannot account for the hemolysis evidence (pink plasma, mixed-field DAT), so it is ultimately excludable, but the phrasing itself is not crisp. Independently, A remains the best-supported answer given the source's specific WBIT framework and the stem's explicit statement that the bedside check was verified correct.

Fully grounded in SRC-003 Node 29: two-independent-identifier requirement (name + unique number such as DOB or MRN) present on wristband/request/specimen (line 143), two-qualified-individuals verification (or one person with a validated electronic system) before initiating transfusion (line 137), bedside matching of armband identifiers to unit tag (line 153), and the 'final check'/last-line-of-defense rationale explaining why any discrepancy must halt the transfusion (lines 133-135). All distractors are cleanly excluded: B contradicts the stated single-person-insufficient standard; C proceeding on partial (2 of 3) identifier match is unsafe under the source's exact-match framework; D dismisses a real identifier error as 'likely transcription' contrary to the stated purpose of the check; E (relabeling the tag to force a match) bypasses verification rather than resolving it. Minor stylistic note: the stem supplies three identifier fields (name, DOB, MRN) where the source frames 'two identifiers' as name plus one unique number (DOB or MRN); a very literal reading could technically treat name+DOB as the two required identifiers (both matching) with MRN as a third/incidental field. This does not change the correct disposition (any mismatch on the unit tag vs. wristband must halt transfusion per the source's overriding safety principle), so it does not create a viable alternative answer among the five options. The specific resolution pathway ('notify the blood bank') is a reasonable, standard extrapolation from the stated principles rather than a verbatim-quoted procedure, but it does not contradict anything in the sources.

SRC-043 (line 81) explicitly states 'TRALI (inflammatory, noncardiogenic) vs. TACO (hydrostatic, volume overload)' and (line 83) 'Pathophysiology of IgA deficiency (Allergic) vs. cytokines (Febrile)'; SRC-032 and SRC-033 corroborate TRALI/TACO/hemolytic as leading FDA-reported transfusion fatalities. The vignette (acute dyspnea/hypoxemia/bilateral infiltrates, no volume overload, plasma transfusion) maps cleanly onto TRALI's inflammatory/noncardiogenic mechanism; TACO, allergic, febrile, and hemolytic distractors are each excluded by specific stem details (no volume overload, respiratory not urticarial/febrile/hemolytic picture). Single best answer, no plausible competing choice.

SRC-032 (lines 103-111) gives verbatim NHSN imputability definitions: Definitive (fulfills all case-definition criteria), Probable (transfusion most likely cause), Possible (transfusion cannot be ruled out but other potential causes are likely), Doubtful/Ruled Out (evidence clearly favors or conclusively proves a non-transfusion cause); SRC-033 and SRC-043 corroborate the Near-Miss definition used to exclude option E. The stem's negative DAT/no visual hemolysis/confirmed ABO match correctly excludes a hemolytic mechanism, and 'UTI at least as likely as transfusion' precisely satisfies the 'Possible' definition (competing cause present, not clearly favored to conclusively rule out transfusion, and not established as the single most likely cause required for Probable). Minor low-severity note: the stem's 'at least as likely' phrasing requires careful parsing to distinguish Possible from Probable/Doubtful, but it resolves unambiguously once the sourced definitions are applied.

All three of SRC-032 (line 125), SRC-033 (line 57), and SRC-043 (line 155) state that suspected transfusion-related deaths/fatalities must be reported to FDA (CBER) as soon as possible (oral/electronic) with a written report within 7 days, distinct from Biological Product Deviation reporting which SRC-033 (line 55) and SRC-043 (line 153) place at up to 45 calendar days from discovery via Form FDA 3486. Clerical error as the #1 cause of acute hemolytic reactions is confirmed in SRC-043 (line 79) and SRC-032 (line 127). Option A is clearly the single best, fully sourced answer; distractors B-E are each directly contradicted by the sourced timelines/requirements.

Core mechanism is well grounded: SRC-024 ('The H Gene Necessity': FUT1 is a prerequisite for A/B expression; without H substance/L-fucose, A/B transferases have no substrate) and SRC-010 (FUT1 encodes alpha-2-L-fucosyltransferase adding L-fucose to form H antigen) directly support the keyed answer C. SRC-043's 'FUT1 (erythroid) vs FUT2 (secretory)' line supports refuting distractor B. However, several stem/rationale details are not verifiable in these four sources and appear to be reasonable but unsourced elaboration: (1) the specific term 'amorphic/silent alleles' tied to FUT1/Bombay (SRC-043 only has a generic 'role of silent alleles' curriculum bullet, not linked to FUT1 or Bombay); (2) the clinical detail that parental consanguinity is characteristically associated with Bombay phenotype; (3) the claim that reactivity occurs 'at both room temperature and after the antihuman globulin phase' -- none of this phase-specific serologic detail is stated in the sources (only that anti-H 'reacts with all O cells'); (4) distractor D's specific claim that Se-loss cannot convert H into Lewis antigen is inferred from a one-line curriculum bullet (SRC-043: 'Le, Se, and H gene interactions') rather than explicit refutation. None of this changes that C is clearly the single best/only defensible answer (A2 cells virtually always react with anti-A, ruling out option A directly from the stem, which slightly weakens A as a distractor). Given the gaps in explicit sourcing for scenario embellishments, mark partial rather than full pass.

The central fact is well grounded: SRC-024 explicitly lists 'Extra Reactivity: Caused by acquired B phenotype (intestinal disease), cold autoantibodies, or rouleaux (multiple myeloma)' and 'Forward vs. Reverse: Forward typing discrepancies are usually RBC problems (subgroups, disease); reverse typing discrepancies are usually serum problems,' which supports keying D and explains why B/C/E are wrong-mechanism distractors, and SRC-024's leukemia/missing-antigen line supports ruling out C. However, the detailed serologic-logic rationale for distractor A (that a true AB/B-subgroup patient could not produce anti-B, i.e., Landsteiner's law/isohemagglutinin suppression) is standard immunohematology knowledge not explicitly stated anywhere in these four sources -- it is a reasonable but unsourced inferential leap used to eliminate a plausible-sounding option. Separately, this question is filed under the blueprint node 'ABH Antigens > Biochemistry,' but its actual content and cognitive demand (resolving an ABO forward/reverse discrepancy pattern and localizing it to antigen- vs serum-side) is diagnostic/interpretive rather than testing the biosynthetic biochemistry of ABH antigens (H/A/B transferase pathway) tested in Q0 -- a plausible node/content mismatch worth flagging even though the fact itself (acquired B) does reflect an antigen-modification mechanism.

Strongly and almost verbatim grounded: SRC-024 states 'Bombay Phenotype (Oh): Presents as a group O discrepancy; these patients have a potent anti-H that reacts with all O cells, requiring transfusion with rare Oh units,' which directly supports the stem setup and keyed answer B. Distractor E is grounded by SRC-010's statement that TPE is a primary treatment for TTP via ADAMTS13-inhibitor removal (not an antibody-clearance technique for enabling incompatible transfusion), and distractor C's rebuttal is reasonably grounded by SRC-024's discrepancy-resolution methods (cell washing/extended incubation as in-vitro techniques, not a therapeutic maneuver). Two quality concerns: (1) blueprint-mismatch -- this question tests transfusion-management strategy for a known Bombay patient, not the biochemistry of ABH antigen synthesis specified by the 'Biochemistry' blueprint node; it would fit better under a clinical-management or transfusion-practice node. (2) duplicate-coverage -- Q0 and Q2 both center on the same single-sentence source fact about Bombay/anti-H (SRC-024's one Bombay sentence is the sole direct source for both items), so two of the three questions in this small set draw their core content from the same thin, one-sentence source anchor, testing different skills (mechanism vs. management) but reducing topical breadth within the set.

The core fact - RHAG is a 'regulator gene' for Rh antigens that does not code for the antigen but controls its expression, contrasted with KLF1 as the Lutheran regulator gene - is directly supported by SRC-003 (Subdomain Node 26, Definitions: 'Regulator Genes: Genes that do not code for the antigen itself but control its expression (e.g., RHAG for Rh antigens; KLF1 for Lutheran)'). That single parenthetical line is the entire source basis for the question. The elaborate clinical vignette built on top of it - 'markedly weakened expression of all Rh system antigens,' DNA sequencing 'confirming intact, non-mutated RHD and RHCE coding sequences,' the specific magnitude/pattern of antigen depression consistent with a regulator-type Rh-mod phenotype - is not stated anywhere in the four sources; it is medically accurate outside knowledge grafted onto a thin source hook. Given the instruction to treat thin/extrapolated support as partial rather than pass, this is graded partial. Within the four given options the deduction is still unambiguous (RHD/RHCE ruled out by stem, KLF1 explicitly tied to Lutheran not Rh in the source), so it remains a single best answer matching the key.

Near-verbatim source match: SRC-004 (Subdomain Node 41, Clinical Applications: Sickle Cell Disease) states testing is 'Essential for providing extended antigen matching and identifying Rh variants that appear as conventional D by serology but are at risk for allo-anti-D,' which directly grounds the stem, the correct answer, and its rationale. SRC-028's DNA Analysis section ('used for blood group gene mapping and RHD genotyping') reasonably supports RHD genotyping as the follow-up test named in option B. Distractors A, C, D, E are all correctly identified as unsupported or contradicted by the source framing. One process flag: this question is filed under the blueprint node 'Rh Blood Group System - General Information & Nomenclature,' but its content and its own source locator ('Node 41 - Clinical Applications: Sickle Cell Disease') are squarely clinical-application/molecular-testing content, not general Rh nomenclature - a plausible blueprint placement mismatch, though not a factual defect.

Directly supported: SRC-004 (Subdomain Node 41, Clinical Applications: Prenatal Care) states 'Testing cell-free fetal DNA (cffDNA) in maternal plasma to determine fetal RhD status non-invasively, guiding RhIg administration,' which matches the stem and keyed answer essentially verbatim. Distractors (amniocentesis, blanket RhIg administration, maternal antibody screen alone) are all correctly and non-controversially identified as inferior/incorrect per the source and basic transfusion medicine logic; no invented facts detected. As with Q1, the topic fits under 'Clinical Applications: Prenatal Care' per its own source locator rather than the blueprint node 'Rh Blood Group System - General Information & Nomenclature' this batch is nominally targeting - a placement/blueprint concern rather than a correctness concern.

All asserted facts check out in SRC-032 (essentials-of-transfusion-medicine-and-blood-management.md): restrictive threshold 7-8 g/dL (line 55), TRICC as evidence basis (line 71), ACS/severe thrombocytopenia as named exceptions to restrictive strategy (line 79), EPO/iron placed under 'Preoperative Anemia' for elective surgery (line 63), and the fresh-vs-stored RBC 'storage lesion' distractor with no significant survival difference in large trials (line 41). Option A is the only defensible answer given the sources; B, C, D are each cleanly contradicted. Two quality concerns: (1) BLUEPRINT-MISMATCH — the assigned node is 'Regulation of Systematic Oxygen Delivery' (S3.b.i, C), which implies oxygen-delivery physiology (DO2/VO2, compensatory mechanisms, 2,3-DPG, P50, oxygen extraction). None of these appear anywhere in the four approved sources; the content actually tested here is drawn entirely from Subdomain Node 17 (Patient Blood Management) and Node 16 (Massive Transfusion Protocols) of SRC-032, i.e., administrative/clinical-practice transfusion-threshold content, not oxygen-delivery regulation physiology. This mismatch is systemic across all 3 candidates, not an isolated tagging slip. (2) Minor AMBIGUOUS-STEM — the patient is actively bleeding, which independently justifies transfusion regardless of ACS status; the stem's framing ('a level that would not typically trigger transfusion... in a non-bleeding patient') partly conflates the bleeding confound with the ACS-exception rationale being tested, though no distractor exploits this so the key is unaffected.

All facts verified in SRC-032: expected increment of ~1 g/dL per unit (line 73), leukoreduced units must retain ≥85% of original RBC content (line 145), clerical errors as the primary cause of acute intravascular hemolytic reactions (line 127), and citrate toxicity/ionized-calcium monitoring tied specifically to massive transfusion protocols (lines 21, 31), not routine 2-unit transfusion. A 0.5 g/dL rise after 2 units is well below the ~2 g/dL expected and 'further evaluation is warranted' is the only option that neither understates (B) nor overreaches to a specific unproven cause (C, D, E). Same systemic BLUEPRINT-MISMATCH concern as question 0: this tests PBM/hemovigilance/component-manufacturing facts (Subdomain Nodes 17-19 of SRC-032), not oxygen-delivery regulation physiology, which is the stated blueprint node and does not appear to be covered anywhere in the four approved sources.

Facts verified: restrictive threshold 7-8 g/dL and TRICC evidence basis (SRC-032, lines 55/71), the 'Why give 2 when 1 will do?' single-unit campaign (SRC-032, line 59), PBM reducing 'blanket orders' for 2 units (SRC-053, Patient Blood Management (PBM) section), and TEG/TEM guiding decisions about transfusing specific coagulation factors rather than empirical recipes (SRC-027, line 155). Hgb 6.8 g/dL is below the restrictive threshold with no ACS exception present, so single-unit transfusion with reassessment (A) is correctly keyed; B misapplies 'treat the patient not the value' as a reason to withhold once the threshold trigger is met, C and D contradict the sourced PBM/restrictive-strategy facts, and E conflates coagulation-factor guidance with RBC transfusion-threshold decisions. Same systemic BLUEPRINT-MISMATCH as questions 0-1: content is PBM administrative/clinical-practice material (SRC-032 Node 17, SRC-053, SRC-027), not oxygen-delivery-regulation physiology as the assigned blueprint node implies, and no source among the four approved sources contains oxygen-delivery physiology content (DO2/VO2, 2,3-DPG, P50, extraction ratio) to ground that node.

Core teaching content is explicitly and strongly grounded: SRC-020 and SRC-059 both define Partial D as a qualitative epitope loss from an RHCE/RHD hybrid gene carrying real alloanti-D risk, and SRC-059 states verbatim that 'Recipients with partial D (e.g., DVI) should be considered Rh-negative for transfusion and RhIg candidacy to prevent sensitization to the missing epitopes' -- this directly supports key B. Distractor A is well-refuted because SRC-020/059 state weak D patients are 'unlikely to make anti-D' (opposite of A's claim), distractor C is refuted because complete RHD deletion is the ordinary D-negative mechanism (not the hybrid-gene finding in the stem) and 'inevitably' overstates the source's 80-90% immunization-rate figure, distractor E matches the source's Position Effect definition (Dce/dCe) as a genuinely distinct mechanism, and D (passive anti-D from RhIg) is unsupported by any stem detail. Minor low-severity note: SRC-020 states initial testing reagent blends are designed so that DVI-type partial D types NEGATIVE (not 'weak/discrepant') in recipients; the stem's premise of weak/discrepant immediate-spin reactivity is plausible for other partial D subtypes but is not the specific DVI behavior described in the source, a small unverified scene-setting detail that does not affect the correct answer or teaching point.

Correct answer A is essentially verbatim from SRC-056: 'Although p-negative blood is ideal, it is extremely rare (~1:200,000). Transfusion should not be withheld in urgent cases, as standard blood may be tolerated once the patient is warmed.' Stem details (post-viral acute intravascular hemolysis/hemoglobinuria in a child, positive Donath-Landsteiner test, pan-reactivity except rare p/Pk = anti-P) match SRC-056's PCH/anti-P definitions closely. Distractor B contradicts the same 'do not withhold' guidance; C is refuted because anti-I (not anti-P) is the CAS specificity and does not produce the p/Pk exception pattern; D is refuted by the explicit 'PCH Isotype' board-trap bullet stating the PCH antibody is IgG despite cold reactivity; E is refuted because the source only describes enzymes enhancing in-vitro anti-P detection, not donor-unit pretreatment for compatibility. Low-severity flag: the source's single 'DAT Results' bullet (C3-positive/IgG-negative 'because the IgM antibody dissociates during washing') sits in a section covering both CAS and PCH without explicitly labeling which entity it describes, so applying it to the PCH case in the stem, and using it in option D's rationale to explain the DAT pattern, is a reasonable but not fully explicit synthesis rather than a verbatim PCH-specific statement; this does not change the correct answer or the overall grounding quality.

SRC-024 line 7 states verbatim: 'Mature RBCs lack a nucleus and mitochondria, relying on anaerobic glycolysis (Embden-Meyerhof pathway) for 90% of their ATP' — this directly and fully grounds the stem and the keyed answer C. Distractors B (HMP shunt as major ATP source), D (Krebs cycle in retained mitochondria), and E (nuclear transcription) are all correctly falsified by the same source sentence (anucleate, amitochondrial, glycolysis-dependent). Distractor A (beta-oxidation) is not explicitly named in the source but is a standard, non-contradicted extension of 'lack mitochondria' and is a reasonable distractor. No other defensible answer exists given the source; independently I also select C. This question fits the 'Regulation of Energy Metabolism' node well, since it explains the structural constraint on RBC ATP production.

SRC-024 directly supports the content: 'Shift to the Left: Occurs when 2,3-DPG is depleted (storage lesion); oxygen affinity increases, meaning less oxygen is released to tissues' plus the P50 definition ('partial pressure of oxygen at which hemoglobin is 50% saturated; a measure of oxygen affinity'). This unambiguously supports B (leftward shift, decreased P50, increased affinity, reduced O2 release) and correctly falsifies A/C (wrong direction), D (contradicts source), and E (internally inconsistent affinity/P50 pairing). The CPDA-1 35-day storage detail is also correctly sourced (line 79: 'CPDA-1 = 35 days'). Independently I also select B — no other defensible answer exists. However, the question is tagged to blueprint node 'Regulation of Energy Metabolism' (S3.c.i.3), yet it tests almost entirely the oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation curve/P50 concept (an 'Oxygen Delivery' topic) and the storage-lesion consequence of 2,3-DPG depletion, not the regulation of glycolytic/energy-metabolism pathways themselves (e.g., enzyme kinetics, feedback control, Rapoport-Luebering shunt regulation, which are never mentioned). This is better classified under Oxygen Delivery or Preservation/Storage Lesion than 'Regulation of Energy Metabolism,' so I flag a moderate blueprint-topic mismatch even though the factual content is well supported.

None of the four approved sources (SRC-003, SRC-027, SRC-012, SRC-023) contain any content on the Rapoport-Luebering shunt, 2,3-DPG, glycolysis, or oxygen-dissociation-curve physiology (confirmed by full read + keyword search for DPG/Rapoport/glycol/Embden/Meyerhof/oxygen affinity/dissociation curve/ATP/storage lesion). The single hit across all four sources is an unrelated mention of 'ATP-binding cassette transporters' for Jr/Lan blood group antigens in SRC-003, which the question's own source_references section correctly labels as tangential/non-supportive. Every substantive fact in the stem, key, explanation, and all five option rationales is entirely unsupported by this source set, despite being factually accurate per general hematology knowledge. The question is well-constructed with a single defensible answer (A) and no ambiguity in the distractors, but it fails the source-grounding requirement completely for this specific approved corpus.

None of the four approved sources contain any content on the hexose monophosphate/pentose phosphate shunt, G6PD, NADPH, or glutathione metabolism (confirmed by full read + keyword search for NADPH/NADH/glutathione/G6PD/hexose/pentose/Heinz/bite cell). Zero relevant hits found in any of the four files. The question's own source_references section acknowledges SRC-003 has no content on this topic. Every fact in the stem (G6PD deficiency clinical vignette, Heinz bodies, bite cells), the key, and all option rationales is ungrounded in this specific source set, though independently correct per standard hematology teaching. The item itself is well-constructed with a single unambiguous best answer (A) and a legitimate classic distractor (E, reduced glutathione vs. its direct synthetic precursor), but fails source support entirely for the approved corpus provided.

SRC-010 (III. Domain: RBCs and RBC Components) lists 'Subdomains: Red Cell Production, Oxygen Delivery, Metabolism, Preservation, Immunology, and Blood Group Systems' followed by 'Major Concepts: Erythropoietin regulation; hemoglobin-oxygen dissociation curves; red cell storage lesions; biochemistry of carbohydrate and protein blood groups' as two adjacent but separately-worded lists — the source never states an explicit one-to-one correspondence table pairing each subdomain to a specific concept. The question's explanation asserts 'the source curriculum map pairs each RBC subdomain with its governing major concept' as if this were an explicit stated mapping; in fact it is a positional inference by the question-writer (and note the 6 subdomains vs. 4 concepts don't even align cleanly — 'Metabolism' has no corresponding concept in the list, meaning the lists are not strictly parallel/1:1 as claimed). Each individual named fact (erythropoietin↔production, dissociation curve↔the domain, storage lesion↔preservation, antigen biochemistry↔immunology/blood group) does appear verbatim in SRC-010, and the leukoreduction distractor fact is verbatim in SRC-044 (Domain IV), so nothing is fabricated — but the specific subdomain-to-concept ASSIGNMENT that the stem/key/all-option-rationales depend on is an unstated inference dressed up as an explicit source mapping. Independently, general hematology knowledge (not elaborated anywhere in these 4 sources beyond the bare phrase) supports A as by far the best answer among the five options, so the key is still defensible, but the grounding is thinner than the explanation claims. The question's own metadata (support_confidence: low) already signals this weakness. Minor secondary note: option E (leukoreduction) is a comparatively easy, transparently-unrelated distractor (component modification vs. physiologic O2 delivery), making it less discriminating than B/C/D.

Fully and directly grounded in SRC-044's Domain II 'Hemotherapy Triggers & Strategy' table: 'Restrictive Strategy: Hb <7 g/dL for stable hospitalized patients (ICU/Orthopedic). Liberal Strategy: Hb <8 g/dL for patients with symptomatic anemia or pre-existing cardiovascular disease,' plus the Platelet (<10,000/uL prophylactic), Plasma (PT/aPTT >1.5x mid-normal with active bleeding), and Cryoprecipitate (fibrinogen <100 mg/dL) trigger values used in the distractors — all verbatim-matched. The patient (known CAD, symptomatic dyspnea/fatigue, Hb 7.5 g/dL, normal platelets/PT/aPTT, no bleeding) unambiguously falls under the liberal (<8 g/dL) category, making A the single defensible answer; B misapplies the restrictive threshold, and C/D/E cite real but inapplicable trigger criteria (coag, platelet, cryo) that don't fit this patient's normal labs. No invented facts. One structural note: the tested content (RBC transfusion Hb thresholds) is sourced from SRC-044's Domain II 'Clinical Practice' hemotherapy-trigger table rather than from the Domain III 'RBCs and RBC Components > Oxygen Delivery' subdomain named in the blueprint node; it is defensible as in-scope only via the node's 'and Use of Red Cells' clause and the teaching point's framing of thresholds as reflecting cardiovascular oxygen-delivery reserve — a reasonable but not perfectly tight fit to the stated node, worth a low-severity mismatch flag rather than a support failure.
