Separately, there is a scientific-plausibility wrinkle not addressed by the sources: mixed-field agglutination after simple RBC transfusion reflects residual anucleate donor RBCs, so DNA comparison across nucleated tissues (blood WBCs vs. buccal) more naturally detects leukocyte/stem-cell chimerism than isolated donor RBC persistence; the source itself conflates these under 'artificial chimerism,' so this is a source-level simplification rather than an invented fact by the question-writer.

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events

audit.verdict_notes: Both underlying facts are explicit in SRC-043 Neonatal Transfusion Logic: hyperkalemia risk 'especially with older irradiated units' and 'small-volume aliquots (10-15 mL/kg)' for neonatal transfusion. Combining them into a management strategy is a reasonable synthesis. The only extrapolation is the specific phrase 'irradiated as close to the time of transfusion as possible' — the source ties hyperkalemia to unit/donation age ('older irradiated units') but does not separately state or emphasize minimizing the irradiation-to-transfusion interval as its own lever; this is standard clinical practice but not verbatim source content. Distractors are solidly grounded: B misapplies the leukoreduction-vs-irradiation distinction (SRC-043), C misapplies CMV-seronegative selection which SRC-012 Node 52 frames as a separate CMV-specific strategy, D contradicts the mandatory-irradiation/TA-GVHD-fatality fact, and E directly contradicts the stated small-aliquot recommendation. No other option is defensible, so the key stands as the single best answer.

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events

audit.verdict_notes: Core teaching point is grounded: SRC-010 states 'TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes,' and SRC-038 lists congenital immunodeficiencies as an HPCT indication and states TA-GVHD is prevented by lifelong irradiation of cellular components. However, the option A rationale text ('engraft, proliferate, and attack the recipient's tissues because her severe congenital immunodeficiency prevented rejection of the foreign donor cells') is a physiologic elaboration not verbatim in any of the four sources -- none of the four sources contains an explicit step-by-step TA-GVHD pathophysiology paragraph (unlike PNH/HNA, which do get dedicated mechanism sections). The clinical vignette details (12-day onset, maculopapular rash, watery diarrhea, transaminitis, pancytopenia) are standard TA-GVHD teaching but are also not stated anywhere in these four sources. Distractors B (TRALI/HNA-3a per SRC-004), C (clerical/ABO error per SRC-032), and D (TACO per SRC-010) are each well grounded and clearly differentiated. A is unambiguously the best answer among the five options; no equally defensible alternative exists.

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events

audit.verdict_notes: SRC-038 defines chimerism monitoring/STR analysis, but only in the explicit context of 'evaluate engraftment and monitor for relapse' after HSCT -- the source never states this technique is used to confirm TA-GVHD in a transfusion (non-HSCT) recipient. The question's explanation candidly frames this as an 'applied' extension, which is medically accurate (STR chimerism is the real-world confirmatory test for TA-GVHD) but is an inference beyond the literal source text, so it should not be scored a clean 'pass.' The four distractors are each individually well-grounded in their stated source definitions (DAT -> SRC-032; Donath-Landsteiner -> SRC-010; CD55/CD59 flow -> SRC-004; CD34 enumeration -> SRC-038) and are clearly mismatched to the TA-GVHD-donor-chimerism question, making them strong, non-overlapping distractors. A remains the unambiguous best answer; no other option plausibly detects donor-derived cells.

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events

audit.verdict_notes: SRC-038 literally states 'HSCT recipients must receive irradiated or pathogen-reduced standard blood components (RBCs, Platelets) for life' -- this patient is pre-transplant ('awaiting' HPCT), not yet an HSCT recipient, so the source text does not unambiguously extend the irradiation mandate to the pre-transplant immunodeficient period. The question's exclusion of option D ('irradiation is only required...after engraftment') therefore rests on an inference (that congenital T-cell immunodeficiency itself, independent of transplant timing, mandates irradiation now) rather than an explicit source statement; this is standard real-world blood-bank practice but is the exact point being tested, so the gap is more consequential here than in Q0/Q1. Similarly, the implicit premise that leukoreduction (option B) is insufficient to prevent TA-GVHD is not explicitly stated by any source (leukoreduction specs in SRC-032 and the HPC-graft-leukoreduction rule in SRC-038 are both silent on RBC/platelet leukoreduction's inadequacy for TA-GVHD prevention) -- again true and well-established outside these sources, but not verbatim grounded. Option E is very well supported by SRC-038's explicit 'IRRADIATION ERROR' board trap (HPC graft must never be irradiated), and option C (CMV-seronegative addresses CMV risk, a distinct indication) is well grounded by SRC-038. Despite the grounding gap on excluding D, A is still clearly the best real-world answer and no other option is defensible as 'most appropriate.'

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events

audit.verdict_notes: Core mechanism is well-grounded: SRC-038/SRC-040 both state HSCT recipients must receive irradiated standard blood components for life to prevent TA-GVHD while the HPC graft itself must never be irradiated because irradiation destroys the cells' proliferative/engraftment capacity (SRC-040 'Processing Rules' and 'Irradiation Necessity'; SRC-038 'IRRADIATION ERROR'). SRC-038 also states allogeneic recipients require immunosuppression to prevent donor T-cells from attacking host tissue, and that HLA disparity is the primary rejection barrier (supporting distractor D as the inverse/normal-host scenario). All four distractors are individually source-grounded as real but inapplicable phenomena: B matches the PRCA mechanism verbatim (isoagglutinins lysing donor erythroid precursors, SRC-038/SRC-040), C matches the GVN-effect definition (SRC-038 Core Concepts), D matches the HLA-barrier statement (SRC-038), and E is correctly identified as unsupported/reversed. Minor nuance: option A attributes the sustained immunosuppression specifically to 'the transplant conditioning regimen,' whereas the sources actually cite ongoing post-transplant immunosuppressive drugs (calcineurin inhibitors) as maintaining the immunosuppressed state weeks post-transplant, alongside conditioning-induced marrow ablation; the synthesis is a reasonable, standard board-level inference but not a verbatim single-sentence source statement. No other option is defensible; A is clearly the single best answer.

questions.domain: 6. Hazards of Transfusion: Specific Adverse Events

audit.verdict_notes: SRC-038 explicitly states chimerism monitoring assesses 'the ratio of donor to recipient cells (often using STR analysis) to evaluate engraftment and monitor for relapse' - but this is stated for general post-transplant monitoring, not explicitly for diagnosing TA-GVHD via skin/marrow biopsy tissue infiltration. The question's application of this technique to biopsy tissue for TA-GVHD confirmation is a reasonable, standard real-world clinical extrapolation (and the question's own metadata self-labels support_confidence as 'moderate,' appropriately hedged) but is not verbatim/explicitly stated in the four approved sources, so it is graded partial rather than pass. All four distractors are, however, precisely and correctly grounded: DAT (B) matches SRC-040's DIIHA workup description (EDTA RBCs, IgG/C3d) and is unrelated to tissue infiltration; CD34 enumeration (C) and CFU assay (E) both match SRC-038/SRC-040's potency/dose testing descriptions verbatim (CFU = 'gold standard,' 2-week culture; CD34 = rapid flow-cytometry surrogate); virtual crossmatch (D) matches SRC-028's Expert Note definition verbatim (recipient HLA antibody profile via solid-phase assay vs. donor HLA type, pre-transplant compatibility). Because all four distractors are clearly inapplicable to diagnosing tissue-infiltrative donor chimerism, A remains the unambiguous single best answer despite the extrapolation concern on the correct-answer rationale.

Core facts are grounded: SRC-002 Node 47 states 'Vitamin K Deficiency: Leads to the production of nonfunctional Factors II, VII, IX, and X (PIVKA...)' (matches option A's factor list and PIVKA definition verbatim), the DIC bullet matches option C's distractor almost verbatim, and the Liver Disease bullet ('decreased synthesis of nearly all clotting factors except Factor VIII and vWF') matches option E verbatim. However, the specific mechanistic term 'gamma-carboxylation' used in the stem-answer explanation and correct-option text does not appear anywhere in any of the three source files (grepped all three, zero hits for 'carboxyl'). It is standard, uncontested textbook knowledge consistent with the PIVKA acronym, but it is an added detail not literally present in the cited sources. Also, labeling warfarin specifically as a 'vitamin K antagonist' whose action produces PIVKA factors requires bridging SRC-002's 'Vitamin K Deficiency' bullet with SRC-051's separate 'PCC ... urgent reversal of Vitamin K Antagonists (VKAs) like warfarin' bullet — a reasonable but source-spanning inference. Option D's 'autoantibody' framing for factor VIII inhibitors is also not explicitly stated in source (source only says 'high-titer inhibitors'), a minor gap since D is a distractor. None of this changes the correct answer: A is clearly the only defensible choice given the grounded distinctions among DIC, liver disease, and factor VIII inhibitors.

The individual supporting facts are each grounded: SRC-051 Node 39 states factors 'like VII, VIII, and IX have short half-lives (<24 hours) and require frequent dosing' and SRC-002 Node 47 states PCC 'is preferred over FFP due to its lower volume and faster normalization of the INR.' However, the correct answer's core causal claim — that PCC delivers factor VII specifically, whose transient presence explains the INR rebound — requires assuming four-factor PCC's composition (factors II, VII, IX, X), a fact that is never stated anywhere in any of the three source documents (no mention of PCC's factor content). This is standard, correct hematology knowledge, but it is an invented linking fact relative to these sources, not merely a paraphrase. The explanation also reuses the unsourced term 'gamma-carboxylation' (absent from all three files). The overall 'INR rebound without concurrent vitamin K' teaching point is medically accurate and a common board pearl, but it synthesizes three separate bullets across two source files into a mechanism the sources never explicitly articulate — appropriately reflected by the question author's own 'moderate' (not 'high') support_confidence rating. Despite the grounding gap, option A remains the only medically defensible answer among the five (B, C, D, E are all clearly wrong under the source's own framework), so single_best_answer holds.

Fully and directly grounded. SRC-051 Node 39 explicitly states 'PCC (Prothrombin Complex Concentrate): Treatment of choice for the urgent reversal of Vitamin K Antagonists (VKAs) like warfarin in bleeding patients,' and SRC-002 Node 47 states 'PCC is preferred over FFP due to its lower volume and faster normalization of the INR' — together directly supporting option A over option B in this time-critical ICH scenario. Distractors are each cleanly grounded and clearly distinguished: rFVIIa is tied only to factor VIII inhibitor bypassing therapy in the source (not general VKA reversal), fibrinogen concentrate is described as a cryoprecipitate alternative for fibrinogen replacement, and rhAT is tied only to congenital antithrombin deficiency surgery. No invented or contradicted facts found. A is the unambiguous best answer.

Dual mechanism is fully grounded: SRC-010 ('TPE is a primary treatment for TTP by removing ADAMTS13 inhibitors') plus SRC-031 ('plasma is the treatment of choice because it provides the von Willebrand factor-cleaving protease necessary to break down pathogenic ultra-large vWF multimers'; 'require large volumes of plasma via exchange to replace the ADAMTS13 protease'). Distractors are all traceable: A maps to Node 70 therapeutic platelet apheresis (thrombocytosis indication, not TTP), B maps to cryoprecipitate composition (SRC-031), D is a plausible-sounding but unsupported conflation with platelet-refractoriness alloimmunization (SRC-031 platelet refractoriness section describes HLA/HPA mechanism, correctly distinguished as unrelated to TTP), E maps to Node 67 RPGN immune-complex rationale. No invented facts found. Independently the best answer is clearly C — TTP pathophysiology is well established and matches source language closely.

The core keyed fact — 'citrate toxicity, presenting as hypocalcemia' as a common TPE adverse effect — is directly and explicitly stated in SRC-010 ('Common adverse effects include citrate toxicity, presenting as hypocalcemia'). However, the stem's specific symptom description (perioral tingling, finger twitching) is not present anywhere in the four sources; it is standard outside clinical knowledge (classic hypocalcemia paresthesia/tetany signs) layered onto the source's bare 'hypocalcemia' fact, not something SRC-010/011 elaborate on. This is a minor, clinically accurate embellishment rather than a fabricated or contradicted fact, so I mark source_support partial rather than fail. GBS as a TPE Category I indication is correctly grounded in Node 66. Independently, B is clearly the single best answer — no other electrolyte disturbance listed is associated with TPE/citrate in the sources or in standard practice, and hypocalcemia is the classic explanation for the described symptom complex.

The keyed claim (plasma/FFP is the correct TPE replacement fluid in TTP because it supplies ADAMTS13) is well supported by SRC-031 ('plasma is the treatment of choice... provides the von Willebrand factor-cleaving protease') and SRC-010. Cryoprecipitate composition (fibrinogen, FVIII, FXIII, vWF) is correctly sourced to SRC-031. However, the third source_references entry attributes the 'apheresis replacement fluid selection is a clinical choice between albumin and plasma' framing to SRC-027 (domain-2-clinical-practice-patient-management-study-guide.md), locator 'Domain: Apheresis, Major Concepts' — I read SRC-027 in full and it contains no such section and never mentions albumin or plasma as apheresis replacement fluid; that 'Albumin vs. Plasma' framing actually appears in SRC-010 (blood-banking-and-transfusion-medicine-board-curriculum.md, Domain IV: Apheresis, Major Concepts), which is already cited elsewhere in this question for a different claim. This is a genuine source-attribution/citation error (wrong source_id for a real but misattributed fact), not an invented fact outright, so I mark source_support partial with medium severity given it misleads the audit trail about which approved source actually supports the framing. Independently, D is clearly the single best answer regardless of this citation issue.

Every fact is well-grounded in SRC-023 (exponential/asymptotic kinetics; 1.0 PV ~63-70%; 2.0 PV ~90%; IgM 80% intravascular vs IgG 60% extravascular; ACD-A chelates calcium not the pathogenic substance; albumin described only as low-reaction-risk fluid lacking coagulation factors, no saturable-binding mechanism; IFC/CFC distinguished by access not efficiency). Answer A is clearly and uniquely correct. However this question tests generic TPE removal-kinetics/diminishing-returns principle (SRC-023 Subdomain Node 6, Therapeutic Apheresis) and never mentions FFP/albumin/RhIg product dosing at all - it does not fit the assigned blueprint node 'Fresh Frozen...Plasma > Dosing' under Domain 7 Plasma Components. It is essentially an apheresis-kinetics question mapped onto a plasma-product-dosing node. It also substantially overlaps in content (same diminishing-returns figures) with Q2's distractor C, creating redundant coverage of the same source fact across two of the three candidates for this node.

Fully grounded: SRC-020 states 1 vial (300 mcg) covers 30 mL fetal whole blood, gives the exact rounding rule (>=0.5 up, <0.5 down, always +1 safety vial), and SRC-017 corroborates the KB formula and gives worked rounding examples using the identical method. Arithmetic checks out: 45/30 = 1.5 -> rounds up to 2 -> +1 safety = 3 (option C). Option D's rationale (confusing the 15 mL RBC-coverage figure with the 30 mL whole-blood figure) is plausible and source-supported as a common distractor mechanism. This question is a precise, on-target match for the assigned blueprint node (RhIg dosing).

Well supported by SRC-023: plasma (FFP) is explicitly the replacement fluid of choice for TTP to supply ADAMTS13, standard exchange volume is stated as 1.0-1.5 PV, and the TTP pentad (MAHA, thrombocytopenia, neuro signs, fever, renal dysfunction) plus PLASMIC-score-while-awaiting-ADAMTS13 usage both match the stem's clinical picture point for point. All distractor rationales (albumin fails to supply ADAMTS13; higher/lower PV ranges depart from the stated 1.0-1.5 PV standard; diminishing-returns rationale for the 2.0 PV distractor) are source-grounded. Minor note: like Q0, this question is scenario/apheresis-flavored, but it directly engages the 'Plasma Transfusion and the Use of Albumin' portion of the assigned blueprint node (fluid selection + standard PV dosing), so the fit is markedly better than Q0's.

DIC definition (dual procoagulant/fibrinolytic activation requiring platelet/plasma/cryo replacement) is verbatim-supported by SRC-002 node 47. Cryoprecipitate's composition (concentrated fibrinogen, FVIII, FXIII, vWF) matches SRC-031's definitions section exactly. Distractor B (ADAMTS13/TTP) is correctly distinguished per SRC-031's TTP pearl. Distractor C (PIVKA/vitamin K factors) matches SRC-002 verbatim. Distractor E (citrate/hypocalcemia) matches SRC-032's MTP metabolic complications section. No unsupported or contradicted facts found; single best answer is unambiguous.

Hypofibrinogenemia threshold (bleeding below ~100-150 mg/dL) and cryoprecipitate pool dosing effect (~50-70 mg/dL rise per 10-unit pool) are both verbatim-supported in SRC-031. SRC-043 independently confirms cryo (not FFP) is indicated for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL. Distractor C (CCI = platelet-specific formula) matches SRC-031's diagnostic-approach section exactly. Distractor D's plasma dosing figure (10-15 mL/kg) matches SRC-031 management section. Option E's claim of guaranteed normalization is a reasonable inference from the source's use of 'approximately' rather than an absolute endpoint. No contradictions found.

MTP laboratory triggers (platelets <50,000/uL; plasma if INR >1.5 or aPTT >60s; cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100 mg/dL) are verbatim-supported by SRC-032 node 16, and independently corroborated by SRC-043's 'Cryo for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL' line. The patient's exact values (platelet 62k, INR 1.3, fibrinogen 85) each fall unambiguously on the correct side of the respective thresholds, making cryoprecipitate the single defensible answer. PCC-vs-FFP rationale for distractor D matches SRC-002 node 47 verbatim. No unsupported claims identified.

SRC-031 line 5 explicitly assigns oncotic pressure to albumin, immunity to immunoglobulins, and hemostasis to coagulation factors as distinct plasma-protein functions; SRC-032 line 9 lists oxygen-carrying capacity, oncotic pressure, and hemostasis as separate physiologic goals. Cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen distractor (E) and RBC/oxygen-carrying distractor (D) are both consistent with source definitions elsewhere in SRC-031. Independent read confirms A as the only defensible answer; no competing option is supportable.

Core NIPA mechanism, hapten pattern, immune-complex pattern, and drug-independent autoantibody pattern are all verbatim-supported in SRC-040 (lines 17, 37, 39, 41, 43). However, the stem's specific claim that 'monospecific testing shows the RBCs are coated with IgG, complement (C3d), and albumin' goes beyond the source: SRC-040 line 17 only names 'IgG, Albumin, etc.' as NIPA-adsorbed proteins (not C3d specifically), and SRC-032 line 191 defines 'Monospecific AHG' as reagent against ONLY IgG or ONLY complement -- it does not include an albumin-detecting monospecific reagent in this source set. The stem's premise (a 'monospecific' test simultaneously identifying IgG, C3d, and albumin) is not actually grounded in the sources' own definition of monospecific testing, even though the general NIPA concept (multiple nonspecifically adsorbed proteins, negative eluate) is well supported. This does not change the correct answer -- A is still the only defensible choice among the options given -- but the added clinical/serologic specificity is an invented elaboration beyond source text.

SRC-010 (lines 55, 59) and SRC-031 (lines 27, 77) both directly state that TPE is the treatment of choice for TTP because it supplies ADAMTS13 (the VWF-cleaving protease) while removing inhibitors, and SRC-010 line 55 explicitly frames 'Albumin vs. Plasma' as the key replacement-fluid decision point in apheresis, supporting the albumin distractor. Cryoprecipitate composition (fibrinogen, FVIII, FXIII, VWF) is correctly sourced (SRC-031 line 19) and correctly reasoned as inappropriate (adding VWF substrate without the cleaving protease). Independent read confirms plasma (A) as the unambiguous correct answer.

Core teaching point is well grounded: SRC-002 Node 47 states verbatim that DIC is 'a consumption coagulopathy where both procoagulant and fibrinolytic systems are pathologically active; requires replacement of platelets, plasma, and cryoprecipitate' — matches option A. All four distractor mechanisms are explicitly sourced: dilutional coagulopathy (SRC-032 Node 16: 'hemorrhagic state caused by replacing lost whole blood with only RBCs and crystalloids, leading to depleted factor and platelet levels'), citrate toxicity (SRC-032 Node 16: citrate in plasma/platelets chelates calcium causing hypocalcemia), hypothermia (SRC-032 Node 16: refrigerated components 'exacerbate coagulopathy by reducing enzymatic activity'), and liver disease (SRC-002 Node 47: 'decreased synthesis of nearly all clotting factors'). However, the stem's specific diagnostic lab panel for DIC (thrombocytopenia, prolonged PT/aPTT, low fibrinogen, elevated D-dimer, plus hematuria/oozing/ecchymoses) is not enumerated anywhere in the DIC source passage (a single terse sentence) — this is standard/accurate medical knowledge but exceeds what these four approved sources actually state, so it is flagged as an unsupported (though not contradicted) elaboration. Does not change the correct answer or undermine single-best-answer status.

Fully grounded. SRC-032 Node 16 states the exact MTP laboratory triggers verbatim: 'Platelets are indicated if the count is <50,000/µL; plasma is needed if the INR is >1.5 or aPTT exceeds 60 seconds' and 'replacement with cryoprecipitate is often indicated when [fibrinogen] levels fall below 100 mg/dL,' plus 'maintain [ionized calcium] >1.0 mmol/L.' Checking stem values against thresholds: platelets 62,000 (>50k, no trigger), INR 1.3 (<1.5, no trigger), aPTT 42 (<60, no trigger), fibrinogen 85 (<100, triggers cryo — correct answer), ionized calcium 1.2 (>1.0, no trigger). SRC-031 corroborates hypofibrinogenemia causing microvascular bleeding below 100–150 mg/dL. Every option rationale traces cleanly to source thresholds; no invented facts.

Directly and almost verbatim grounded in SRC-032 Node 16 'Common Board Traps and Distractors': 'Group A Plasma: While AB is the universal plasma, many trauma protocols use Group A plasma for emergent release due to the scarcity of AB donors.' This matches both the correct answer (B) and option A's rationale (AB as traditional universal donor). Options C (Group B) and E (withhold plasma) are not discussed in the source as legitimate emergency-release practices — plausible and not contradicted, but comparatively weak/throwaway distractors versus the real A-vs-B tension the question is testing, a minor low-severity quality note rather than a support failure.

SRC-032 (essentials-of-transfusion-medicine...) directly states: 'Thawed Plasma: Once relabeled, it may be used for up to 5 days but is deficient in labile Factors V and VIII' - this is the core fact and is well grounded, as are the facts used to build distractors D (8-hour/6-hour ACD freeze rule) and E (300-404mL low-volume rule bars FFP production), and C (cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100, from SRC-043). However, option A and the explanation add specificity not verbatim in the source: the source gives a blanket 'deficient in labile Factors V and VIII' statement for the whole 5-day Thawed Plasma category, but does not explicitly describe a time-progressive/dose-response decline within that window (i.e., that a unit at day 4 is worse than a unit at day 1) or explicitly state the unit is kept 'refrigerated' post-relabel (standard real-world knowledge, consistent with the source's cold-chain framework, but not restated for Thawed Plasma specifically in the excerpts read). This is a reasonable, biologically accurate elaboration rather than a fabrication, so severity is low, but it goes beyond what is literally stated. Option A remains clearly the best answer among the five; B, C, D, E are all correctly excluded per source facts.

The INR>1.5 / aPTT>60s thresholds and the <50,000/uL platelet threshold are verbatim from SRC-032, but that sentence ('Laboratory Triggers: Platelets are indicated if the count is <50,000/uL; plasma is needed if the INR is >1.5 or aPTT exceeds 60 seconds') appears specifically under Subdomain Node 16 'Massive Transfusion Protocols (MTP)' as an MTP monitoring/management trigger, not as a general context-free rule for routine pre-procedure plasma decisions. The question applies this MTP-context trigger to a stable, non-bleeding, non-MTP bedside-procedure patient without acknowledging the source frames it under massive-hemorrhage resuscitation. This is a moderate sourcing-context gap - notably the same node's 1:1:1 'Balanced Resuscitation' fact is, by contrast, correctly treated in distractor D as MTP-specific and NOT applicable to this patient, creating an internal inconsistency in how strictly the MTP context is honored across options. The blueprint topic tested is 'Fresh Frozen, Frozen, Cryo-Poor, Thawed & Stored Plasma' product characteristics, not MTP lab-trigger logic, so this question arguably sits closer to the MTP node than the cited blueprint node. That said, given the five options, A is still clearly the best/only defensible answer since B, C, D, and E are each excluded by clearly-sourced facts (platelet/fibrinogen values far from their respective thresholds, 1:1:1 being MTP-specific, and PCC/warfarin reversal being irrelevant to a non-anticoagulated patient).

SRC-012 (Mastery Nodes, Node 52) directly states CMV is a latent virus carried within monocytes and granulocytes, and that prestorage LR (<5x10^6 WBCs) removes CMV-carrying cells, making universal LR 'CMV-safe' and nearly replacing seronegative selection. SRC-002 (Advanced Review, Node 48) confirms amotosalen+UVA cross-links nucleic acid, correctly distinguishing PI technology (option C) from LR's cell-removal mechanism. Distractors B, D, E (cell-free plasma virions, antibody removal, platelet reservoir) are not supported by and are reasonably inconsistent with the source's description of CMV as leukocyte-associated. Locators check out against actual node numbering.

SRC-012 explicitly defines CMV-seronegative units as 'from donors who lack IgG antibodies to CMV,' matching key A. Distractor B (<5x10^6 WBC) is correctly tied to the separate 'CMV-safe' LR strategy per the same node. Distractor C's claim that amotosalen+UVA 'replaces the need for...CMV testing' is directly supported by SRC-002 line 57. Distractor D's NAT claim (reduces window period for HIV/HCV/HBV, not tied to CMV serostatus) is directly supported by SRC-010 (Domain VI, line 87), and the locator label matches the actual source heading. Option E's IgM framing is not itself asserted as a sourced fact -- the rationale correctly reasons from the source's stated IgG-only definition, so no fabrication concern.

Underlying facts are each individually sourced: SRC-012 lists intrauterine transfusion recipients and seronegative pregnant women as risk populations for whom CMV mitigation is critical, and separately states seronegative units are 'reserved for highly specific protocols where safe is not deemed sufficient'; SRC-024 states LR is required to decrease CMV transmission generally; SRC-010 supports irradiation-for-TA-GVHD and RBC pathogen-reduction-still-in-trials distractor eliminations. However, the source never explicitly states the combined recommendation 'seronegative AND leukoreduced together' for IUT -- this is a reasonable but interpretive synthesis of two separate bullet points rather than a single stated fact, so mark partial rather than full pass. Also note: the source_reference locator for TA-GVHD/irradiation is labeled 'Board Curriculum, Domain V - Non-infectious Adverse Effects,' but the actual source heading is 'V. Domain: Hazards of Transfusion' -- a minor mislabeled locator (does not affect the underlying fact's accuracy). Despite the synthesis, given the stem's own explicit premise that seronegative units are reserved for cases like this, A remains the clearly best answer among the options; no other option is defensible.

Key A is the only defensible option: B (filters trapping free plasma virions), D (antibody production), and E (HLA-sensitization causally driving CMV prevention) are all clearly refuted, and C is correctly distinguished from Pathogen Inactivation (SRC-044: 'Utilizes Riboflavin/Psoralen + UV light to disrupt pathogen nucleic acids in platelets/plasma'). However, the central mechanistic premise of the correct-answer explanation -- that 'CMV is a cell-associated (leukocyte-associated) virus that persists latently within donor white blood cells' -- is never stated in any of the 4 sources. SRC-044 ('Leukoreduction: <5x10^6 WBCs/unit; prevents FNHTR, HLA sensitization, and CMV transmission') and SRC-013 ('Universal Leukoreduction: Reduces the risk of CMV, HTLV, and EBV transmission') only assert the outcome, never the WBC-latency mechanism. This is true, standard BB/TM knowledge but is external to the cited sources, so the question's claimed 'high' support_confidence is overstated -- should be 'moderate' at best. Also, this item tests a prevention mechanism, not 'window period,' a partial mismatch with the assigned blueprint node (S8.c.6 Window Period and Risk of Transmission). Distractor D is weak/easily eliminated on its face.

This item most directly matches the assigned blueprint node. Key A correctly generalizes SRC-013's window-period definition ('duration between infection and the time when a test (NAT or serology) can detect the agent') to CMV serologic screening. B is correctly rejected: the sources establish an HIV NAT window of ~7-9 days (SRC-044) but never establish a CMV ID-NAT window of 7-9 days; SRC-038's 'CMV-seronegative donors' phrasing implies CMV screening (where done) is serology-based, not NAT-based, so B conflates two different agents/technologies. D is correctly rejected using SRC-013's mandatory testing list (HBsAg, anti-HBc, HBV DNA, anti-HCV, HCV RNA, anti-HIV-1/2, HIV-1 RNA, anti-HTLV-I/II, WNV RNA, Syphilis -- CMV absent), though the underlying window-period concept is not logically restricted to mandatory-list agents. C and E are unsupported/illogical distractors, correctly eliminated. One caveat: the added specific claim that 'virus can be present in leukocytes' during the CMV window period repeats the same unverified WBC-association assumption flagged in Q0 -- not explicitly stated in sources, though it does not change which option is best. Option C's wording ('antibody assays detect viral antigen rather than antibody') is internally confusing and a weak distractor.

Best-grounded of the three items. SRC-038 states verbatim: 'CMV Transmission: Use of CMV-reduced-risk components (leukoreduced or CMV-seronegative)', directly matching option A, and SRC-010 supports the general neonatal requirement ('Neonates ... require specific blood banking practices, such as the use of dedicated units or CMV-reduced components'). Distractor B (irradiation) is a strong, well-sourced classic trap correctly distinguished via SRC-044/SRC-010's TA-GVHD/T-cell-inactivation framing, distinct from CMV-risk reduction. C is correctly rejected because Pathogen Inactivation is described only for platelets/plasma (SRC-044), never RBCs. D correctly catches the 'reduced-risk' vs. 'eliminated' overstatement. E (washing removes plasma proteins harboring CMV) is unsupported and not mentioned anywhere in the sources as a CMV strategy. Minor concern: like Q0, this item tests component-selection/prevention policy rather than the literal 'window period' topic of the assigned blueprint node, though it is the most clinically on-topic of the three for the broader CMV-transmission-risk theme.

SRC-013 ('Questioning Only: Malaria and vCJD rely solely on donor history because no FDA-approved screening tests exist for donors') and SRC-010 ('Malaria and vCJD rely solely on donor questioning as no FDA-approved screening tests exist') directly support key A. Distractors are all correctly grounded as false: MP-NAT/ID-NAT are described only for agents with defined NAT assays (HIV/HCV/HBV/Zika), supplemental testing (e.g., Western blot) is tied to reactive screening assays that don't exist for vCJD, and leukoreduction's documented benefit in the sources is explicitly limited to CMV/HTLV/EBV (SRC-013 line 63), never extended to prion disease. No fabricated facts found.

The listed mandatory panel (HBsAg, anti-HBc, HBV DNA, anti-HCV, HCV RNA, anti-HIV-1/2, HIV-1 RNA, anti-HTLV-I/II, WNV RNA, syphilis) exactly matches SRC-013's 'Mandatory Testing (Allogeneic)' list, and Regional Babesia NAT matches 'Regional Testing.' Since no vCJD test exists per both sources, key A is well supported. Distractor rationales (NAT is agent/nucleic-acid specific, supplemental testing is agent-specific, syphilis algorithm is syphilis-specific, Babesia NAT is agent-specific) are all consistent with source definitions. Flagging low-severity duplicate-coverage: this item retests the same core fact as Q0 (no FDA-approved vCJD test -> donor history is the only screen), just reframed as a negative-panel interpretation rather than a distinct new fact.

Directly matches SRC-013's Common Board Traps section verbatim in substance: 'Only HIV and HCV look-backs are required by federal law (21 CFR 610.46/47); others are governed by professional guidelines,' and Management section: 'Mandatory Recipient Notification: Legally required for HIV and HCV... For other agents, notification is recommended by AABB/FDA guidance.' The citation '21 CFR 610.46/47' used in option B is taken directly from the source, not invented. Distractors are correctly grounded as false: the 3-day retrieval/quarantine rule is explicitly scoped to reactive HIV/HCV/WNV/ZIKV/T. cruzi tests (vCJD excluded, and no reactive vCJD test exists to trigger it); the RTTI list explicitly enumerates HIV, HBV, HCV, HTLV, Syphilis, WNV, T. cruzis, Babesia, Malaria without vCJD; the donor reentry algorithm is HCV-specific; and 'no action required' overstates the source, which still recommends (non-mandatory) notification via AABB/FDA guidance.

Core facts are grounded: SRC-013 explicitly states WNV is 'typically asymptomatic, but can present with fever, headache, and rarely neuroinvasive disease' and that 'every donation must be tested for... WNV RNA' (mandatory NAT). Distractor B's syphilis/4°C contrast is directly grounded ('transmission is extremely rare due to 4°C storage'). Distractor C (chronic carrier state) and D (transmission only during neuroinvasive disease) are correctly identified as unsupported/contradicted. Minor gap: the stem's explicit causal chain 'the donor was viremic during this [asymptomatic] stage' is a reasonable but not verbatim-stated inference from 'typically asymptomatic' + the Window Period/NAT definitions rather than a directly quoted fact; standard virology knowledge, low-risk elaboration. Independently I also select A as the only defensible answer; B, C, D, E are each clearly contradicted or fabricated per the sources.

Directly grounded by SRC-013: 'ID-NAT is more sensitive for low viral loads and is triggered for WNV during peak activity seasons' and 'MP-NAT tests pools of 6 to 16 samples to balance cost and sensitivity.' Distractor B is correctly refuted since MP-NAT itself is a pooled NAT/TMA-based method (SRC-004 confirms TMA detects WNV RNA), so pooling does not make WNV undetectable. Distractor C (WNV-specific Western blot) is unsupported/fabricated -- source only ties Western blot to HIV supplemental testing. D is fabricated and E is directly contradicted by the 'triggered... during peak activity seasons' language. Minor gap: the specific 'dilutional effect' mechanistic explanation in option A is a standard, reasonable extrapolation of 'balance cost and sensitivity' rather than verbatim source text, but it is scientifically uncontroversial and not contradicted by anything in the sources. Independently I select A as the only defensible answer.

Well-grounded on two explicit SRC-013 statements: 'Retrieval and Quarantine: Must be initiated within 3 calendar days of a reactive HIV, HCV, WNV, ZIKV, or T. cruzi test' and 'Mandatory Recipient Notification: Legally required for HIV and HCV. For other agents, notification is recommended by AABB/FDA guidance,' reinforced by the Common Board Traps bullet citing 21 CFR 610.46/47 limiting mandatory look-back to HIV/HCV. B is directly contradicted (WNV is explicitly listed among agents requiring the 3-day retrieval/quarantine rule). C is directly contradicted (notification legally mandated only for HIV/HCV). D misstates the timeline (3 calendar days, not 24 hours) and falsely claims no guidance recommends notification. E is a fabricated restriction with no support (WNV is not described as plasma-only transmissible; RBC transmission via NAT-reactive WNV donations is the entire premise of the mandatory-testing framework). Independently I select A as the only defensible answer; this is the strongest-grounded and most tightly reasoned of the three questions.

Core fact is squarely and explicitly grounded: SRC-015 'High-Yield Comparison Points: RBC vs. Platelet Contaminants' states 'RBCs are often contaminated by cold-growing Gram-negatives (e.g., Yersinia enterocolitica); Platelets are usually contaminated by Gram-positives from donor skin (e.g., S. aureus)' — this directly supports key C and refutes A. Distractor B is correctly refuted (sources tie leukoreduction to CMV/HTLV/EBV, not bacterial Gram-type filtering). Distractor D is correctly refuted (SRC-013 'Prevention: Volume Diversion' describes 30-40mL diversion as a general collection practice, not something RBC/whole-blood collection omits). Distractor E is correctly refuted (sources attribute the RBC/gram-negative link to cold-growth capability, not the 42-day shelf life per se). All source_references locators verified accurate against actual document headings. Minor concern: the vignette's specific numbers (28-day storage, 45-minute onset, 37.0->39.6C) are narrative embellishment not found in or verifiable against the sources, though they don't affect answer correctness since the tested mechanism is qualitative. Single best answer, no viable competing choice.

Directly and explicitly grounded: SRC-015 'Diagnostic Approach: Suspected TTBI Workup' states 'Stop transfusion; culture both the patient's blood and the remaining component in the bag (not the segments)' — this is a verbatim match for key B and explicitly excludes option A (segments), which is the classic board trap. Options C, D, and E (pilot tube, additive solution, next donation) are not mentioned anywhere in the sources as appropriate specimens and are correctly refuted as unsupported. Locators verified accurate. Minor flag: this question and question_index 2 both hinge substantially on the same underlying fact (culture patient blood + residual bag, not segments), which is embedded as part of option D's correct answer in Q2 — some redundancy across the 3-question set for this node, though Q1 isolates the specimen-selection facet specifically while Q2 tests broader management, so overlap is partial rather than duplicative.

Well grounded by combining two explicit source statements in SRC-015: 'Clinical Presentations: Transfusion-Associated Sepsis (TTBI)' ('high fever [>=2C rise], rigors, tachycardia, and profound hypotension; most common with room-temperature platelets') plus 'Management: Sepsis' ('Immediate supportive care and broad-spectrum antibiotics for the recipient') and 'Diagnostic Approach: Suspected TTBI Workup' ('Stop transfusion; culture both the patient's blood and the remaining component in the bag'). The stem's 2.3C rise with rigors/tachycardia/hypotension matches the sourced TTBI definition precisely, making D the clear best answer. Distractors A (slow rate + continue if fever resolves), B (antihistamine + continue), C (continue transfusion while pursuing hemolysis workup), and E (delay for repeat typing/UA) are all correctly refuted as inconsistent with the sourced directive to stop the transfusion for this presentation. Locators verified accurate. Minor flag: substantial thematic overlap with question_index 1, since D's culture-of-blood-and-residual-unit component restates the specimen fact tested standalone in Q1; the 3-question set could have covered more distinct facets of bacterial contamination (e.g., specific organisms other than Yersinia/S. aureus, PI technology, screening test types) rather than concentrating twice on the stop/culture directive.

SRC-061 Node 15 explicitly states most platelet bacterial contaminants are Gram-positive skin flora (e.g., S. epidermidis) introduced during phlebotomy, contrasts this with cold-growing Gram-negative organisms (e.g., Yersinia) implicated in RBC contamination, states agitation at 20-24C 'promotes bacterial proliferation' (not de novo generation, refuting D), and SRC-010 confirms gamma irradiation targets donor T-lymphocytes for TA-GVHD prevention, not bacterial sterilization (refuting E). All stem facts, the key, and each distractor rationale are grounded. Independently the vignette (skin organism, classic acute TAS with rigors/fever/hypotension) points unambiguously to A.

SRC-061 Node 15 'Common Board Traps' states sampling too early (e.g., <24 hours post-collection) may cause false-negative cultures because bacterial load is too low to detect -- this directly supports A, and the stem's 4-hour sample time is comfortably within that window (arguably a stronger/cleaner example than the source's own <24h boundary). Definitions section supports the Primary Testing vs Point-of-Issue Testing distinction used to refute C, and the Prevention section supports the amotosalen/UVA PI description used to refute D. B is correctly refuted since the source explicitly labels S. epidermidis a Gram-positive organism. Minor flag: the specific '4 hours' and '8 days' scenario numbers are illustrative details not verbatim sourced (source only gives the qualitative '<24 hours' example), a low-severity extrapolation rather than a factual conflict. Independent answer is unambiguously A.

SRC-061 Node 15 'Immediate Action' states verbatim that suspected TAS requires stopping the transfusion, notifying the blood center, and culturing the patient and remaining component including the bag -- exact match for A and for the refutation of E (bag must be cultured, not discarded). SRC-058 Node 32 confirms FNHTR management (antipyretics, no automatic resumption implied) differs from this pathway (refuting B), Node 32 confirms anaphylaxis management is epinephrine/steroids/permanent discontinuation without the culture workup (refuting C), and Node 31 confirms immediate cessation is mandatory for all reactions except isolated urticaria (refuting D, since continuing transfusion is unsafe here). The stem's fever increase (>2C) plus rigors and profound hypotension matches the source's TAS presentation criteria precisely. Independent answer is unambiguously A.

Core claim is supported: SRC-013 L61 states 'Volume Diversion: Collecting the initial 30-40 mL of blood in a side bag to reduce skin-origin bacterial contamination.' However, the specific mechanistic detail asserted in option B and the explanation - that the needle 'dislodge[s] a core (plug) of skin tissue' which is 'explicitly' linked by the source - is NOT present in any of the 4 approved sources (SRC-003, SRC-010, SRC-011, SRC-013). That exact 'skin plug' terminology only appears in a different, non-approved source file (transfusion-medicine-waiha-serology-and-platelet-bacterial-mitigation.md) not part of this approved set. The explanation's claim that the source 'explicitly' describes this mechanism overstates what is actually a terse outcome-only statement. B remains the best of the 5 options regardless (A/C/D/E are all unsupported fabrications), so the key is still correct. Separately, blueprint node S8.f.vi.1 is labeled 'Donor Screening,' but this item tests Volume Diversion, a distinct collection-technique prevention strategy - none of the 4 sources describe a bacterial-specific 'donor screening' strategy (e.g., donor questionnaire on skin lesions), so the node mapping does not match the tested content.

Both underlying facts are directly stated in the sources: SRC-013 'Bacterial Testing: Mandatory for platelets; detected via automated culture or rapid point-of-issue tests' and SRC-013 'Bacterial Contamination: Currently considered the most frequent infectious cause of transfusion-related death, particularly with platelets' (echoed in SRC-010 L89: 'most significant infectious risk, especially in platelets stored at room temperature'). Combining these into 'testing reduces but does not eliminate risk' is a reasonable, defensible synthesis. Minor stretch: the source's formal 'Residual Risk' definition (SRC-013 L9) is explicitly scoped to viral window-period donations, not bacterial-testing residual risk, so invoking 'the source's general concept of residual risk' generalizes slightly beyond the literal definition, though the inference itself (mandatory testing + still-leading cause of death = imperfect risk elimination) is sound. D is clearly the best of the 5 options; A, B, C, and E all assert claims (sterility equivalence, no further monitoring needed, donor exempted from future screening, reflex syphilis NAT) that are unsupported or contradicted by the sources. Blueprint node S8.f.vi.1 ('Donor Screening') does not match this item's actual content, which is about interpreting unit-level bacterial detection test results, not donor screening.

Well grounded in the approved sources. SRC-013: 'Bacterial Testing: Mandatory for platelets; detected via automated culture or rapid point-of-issue tests' directly supports C as the mandated bacterial-specific strategy. SRC-010 L75: 'TA-GVHD is prevented by gamma irradiation of cellular components to inactivate donor T lymphocytes' correctly grounds B as a distractor tied to a different hazard. SRC-013 L63: 'Universal Leukoreduction: Reduces the risk of CMV, HTLV, and EBV transmission' correctly grounds A as tied to different hazards. SRC-013 L59: Pathogen Inactivation is described as 'currently approved' (not 'mandatory') for platelets/plasma, correctly distinguishing D as a real but not-mandated strategy per source wording. SRC-010 L89 confirms platelets are stored at room temperature (implying no routine 4°C storage), supporting rejection of E. C is the single best-supported answer among the five. The only issue is that blueprint node S8.f.vi.1 is specifically 'Donor Screening,' while this item and its correct answer (unit-level bacterial culture/rapid testing) is a laboratory/manufacturing detection strategy, not a donor-screening strategy; none of the 4 approved sources elaborate a bacterial-specific donor-screening practice (e.g., questionnaire content on skin lesions/recent procedures) for this node, so the node assignment does not match the tested content even though the medical content itself is accurate.

The five BPD criteria and the 'regardless of licensure status' rule are verbatim-grounded in SRC-027 lines 105-117, and option A correctly restates all five elements, so the KEYED content is accurate and the distractors (B licensure exemption, C 'outside control', D death-only trigger, E RCA-satisfies-reporting) are all correctly contradicted by the source's NCE 'Reporting' step being distinct from RCA. However, the blueprint node being tested is specifically S8.f.vi.2 'Skin Preparation' (a strategy to reduce posttransfusion sepsis risk), and NONE of the four sources contain any content on skin preparation, arm/phlebotomy-site antisepsis technique, diversion pouches, or bacterial contamination/septic transfusion reactions at all (verified via grep across all four files with zero hits). The entire clinical scenario (bacterial platelet contamination from a failed phlebotomy-site prep procedure causing septic reaction) is invented narrative dressing with no source grounding; the question actually tests general BPD regulatory-reporting rules, a different content area entirely. The source_references section itself admits this ('the skin-antisepsis/bacterial-contamination scenario is constructed... these sources contain no content on skin preparation technique itself').

RCA tool descriptions are well-grounded and corroborated in two sources: SRC-027 ('Fishbone Diagram (Ishikawa): Categorizing contributing factors to visualize the relationship between causes'; FMEA described as prospective/step-by-step; Five Whys as drilling down) and SRC-011 Node 68 (near-identical wording, plus RPN as an FMEA-internal severity/detectability score). Option A (Fishbone) is clearly the best-supported answer and all four distractors are correctly characterized as wrong per source definitions. However, this question again fails to test the blueprint node 'Skin Preparation' (S8.f.vi.2) — it tests generic RCA-tool selection, a Domain 2 quality-management topic unrelated to skin antisepsis strategy. As with question 0, none of the four sources contain any content on bacterial contamination, septic transfusion reactions, or skin preparation (confirmed via grep, zero hits across all four files), so the clinical scenario used to motivate the RCA-tool question is fabricated/unsourced, even though the RCA-tool facts tested are accurate. Self-acknowledged in source_references: 'No source content addresses laboratory or serologic confirmation of bacterial contamination itself, so this question substitutes the closest available sourced content.'

The NCE management sequence (Documentation -> Impact Determination -> Quarantine/Recall -> Investigation -> Root Cause Analysis -> CAPA -> Reporting -> Effectiveness Review) is verbatim-grounded in SRC-027 lines 77-93, and option A (impact determination + quarantine of related in-house components) correctly identifies the two steps that come immediately after documentation and before investigation/RCA/CAPA/reporting; B, C, and E are correctly ordered as occurring later in the sequence. Minor imprecision: option A bundles two sequential steps (Impact Determination, then Quarantine/Recall) into a single 'next step,' which is a reasonable synthesis but not an exact restatement of the source's step-by-step ordering (low-severity ambiguity, does not create a competing correct option). Option D's premise that co-components from the same donation are not implicated is not explicitly addressed in the source but is a reasonable inference from 'secure affected products in-house.' As with the other two questions, this again fails to test the blueprint node 'Skin Preparation' (S8.f.vi.2) — it tests generic nonconforming-event process sequencing. None of the four sources contain any content on bacterial contamination, septic transfusion reactions, or skin preparation strategy (confirmed via grep, zero hits), so the clinical scenario is fabricated scenario-dressing around sourced but topically mismatched quality-process content. Self-acknowledged in source_references: 'The sources contain no clinical treatment content... this question is limited to the sourced quality-process sequence.'

Core claim matches sources directly: SRC-061 defines Initial Volume Diversion as diverting first 30-40mL 'to reduce skin-plug contamination' and states most platelet contaminants are Gram-positive skin flora (e.g., S. epidermidis); SRC-013/SRC-015 corroborate 30-40mL diversion for skin-origin contamination and RBC-vs-platelet Gram-negative/Gram-positive contrast. Option A's phrasing ('plug of epidermis/dermis... dislodged by the phlebotomy needle') is a reasonable elaboration of the source's terse 'skin-plug contamination' language, not an invented fact. Distractors B (PI applied to aliquot), C (leukoreduction targets bacteria), D (refrigeration of aliquot), E (real-time culture gating phlebotomy) are all correctly refuted by source content on PI, leukoreduction's actual purpose (CMV/HTLV/EBV), platelet storage temp (20-24C, not refrigerated), and primary testing being post-collection on the processed unit. No ambiguity; A is clearly the single best answer.

Core answer well grounded: SRC-061's 'Common Board Traps' section states sampling <24h post-collection can cause false-negative cultures due to low bacterial load, and its 'Core Concepts' states platelet storage (20-24C, continuous agitation) promotes bacterial proliferation -- together these fully support option A's reasoning. SRC-015 correctly supports the TAS clinical picture (>2C fever, rigors, hypotension) and the bag-not-segment culture workup. Distractors B (S. epidermidis mislabeled Gram-negative), C (point-of-issue testing conflated with primary culture), D/E (diversion/disinfection claimed to eliminate risk) are all correctly refuted. Minor issue: the SRC-013 source_reference entry claims that source supports 'platelet storage is at conditions promoting proliferation,' but SRC-013 (Subdomain Node 8) never mentions platelet storage temperature/agitation -- that fact is only in SRC-061. This is a citation misattribution (already partly self-flagged via the 'moderate' confidence on that reference) rather than a fabricated fact in the question itself, so it does not undermine the answer's overall grounding, but the SRC-013 citation should be corrected or removed.

Fully grounded: SRC-031 explicitly lists 'initial volume diversion (30-40 mL), skin disinfection, and mandatory bacterial culture or NAT' together as the platelet bacterial-mitigation strategy, directly supporting that diversion/disinfection alone are insufficient. SRC-061 supports PI 'may replace the need for certain bacterial cultures or irradiation' (i.e., not mandatory for every unit regardless of culture status, refuting E) and defines point-of-issue testing as a distinct pre-transfusion strategy for older units (refuting D). Irradiation is tied only to TA-GVHD prevention via granulocyte T-lymphocytes in the sources, never to bacterial reduction, correctly refuting C. No ambiguity; A is the clear single best answer and all distractor rationales are consistent with source content.

Core comparative facts are explicitly grounded: SRC-031 line 39 states 'A single apheresis unit is equivalent to a pool of 4 to 6 whole-blood-derived platelet concentrates' and line 71 states 'Apheresis reduces donor exposure (1 vs. 5 donors)'; SRC-032 lines 151/153 give the WBD (>=5.5x10^10) vs apheresis (>=3.0x10^11) platelet minimums, and line 155 confirms both are 'Maintained at 20C-24C with continuous gentle agitation' under the shared Platelet Component Requirements heading. SRC-031 line 53 states bacterial culture/NAT is a generic platelet-level requirement (not apheresis-restricted, correctly refuting D), and line 57 states PI (amotosalen/UVA) treats 'plasma and platelets' generically (correctly refuting E). Options B and C are correctly identified as contradicted by the same storage-temperature and platelet-count facts. The one unverified addition is the specific causal mechanism asserted in the key rationale and explanation -- 'skin flora introduction at phlebotomy is the principal source of platelet bacterial contamination' / 'multiplying the number of independent venipuncture sites at which skin flora can be introduced' -- which is standard transfusion-medicine knowledge and consistent with the source's listed prevention step of 'skin disinfection,' but is not itself an explicit statement in any of the four sources (skin flora is never named as the mechanism or 'principal source'). This is a reasonable, non-contradicted inference rather than a fabricated or contradicted fact, so it only downgrades support to partial rather than fail. Checked SRC-020 and SRC-025 for related or conflicting content; neither addresses apheresis-vs-WBD bacterial contamination risk, so no contradiction found there. Independently, A is clearly the single best answer given the explicit donor-exposure/pooling facts, and B, C, D, E are each directly contradicted by explicit source statements (same storage temp; apheresis has higher, not lower, platelet count; bacterial testing and PI are described generically for platelets/plasma, not as apheresis-exclusive steps), so no other option is defensible. Question node fit: this squarely addresses S8.f.vi.4 (apheresis vs WBD platelets as a sepsis-risk-reduction strategy).

The stem's factual claims (10.5 mL/kg max collection inclusive of samples; proportional reduction of blood and anticoagulant volume for donors <110 lb) are directly and verbatim confirmed in SRC-003 Node 30 ('Whole Blood Volume'). However, the keyed answer (A) asserts a specific physiologic mechanism -- 'total circulating blood volume scales with body size... limits the fraction of a donor's blood volume removed' -- that is never stated in SRC-003 or any of the four sources; the source gives the rule but not this explanatory rationale. It is standard, medically uncontroversial physiology and is clearly the best of the five options (B misattributes citrate/anticoagulant infusion into the donor during whole-blood collection, which SRC-043 ties only to apheresis; C, D, E are unsupported or reversed), so answer_agrees=true and single_best_answer=true, but the 'explanation_correct' language overstates how directly this specific mechanistic tie-in is grounded in the cited sources -- it is inferred/outside knowledge layered onto a sourced rule, not itself quoted or paraphrased from the text.

Fully grounded in SRC-003 Node 30: allogeneic female Hb >=12.5 g/dL (Hct >=38%), allogeneic male Hb >=13.0 g/dL (Hct >=39%), and autologous Hb >=11.0 g/dL with no age limit, explicitly stated as 'less stringent' than allogeneic. The donor's 11.2 g/dL clears the 11.0 g/dL autologous floor. Every distractor rationale maps cleanly onto a real source fact (wrong threshold substituted, or a fabricated eligibility mechanism correctly identified as fabricated). No invented or contradicted facts; single best answer is unambiguous.

The core deferral-duration facts are verbatim from SRC-003 Node 30 ('High-Yield Specific Deferrals: Medications' -- Proscar/Propecia/Accutane 1 month, Avodart 6 months, Soriatane 3 years, Tegison Permanent), and the key (permanent deferral for Tegison regardless of elapsed time) is unambiguously correct and clearly the single best answer; all four distractors are explicitly and correctly tied to the other listed drugs' real deferral periods. The only ungrounded addition is the stem/rationale pairing brand names with generic names (etretinate=Tegison, and by extension acitretin=Soriatane, dutasteride=Avodart, isotretinoin/finasteride=Accutane/Proscar/Propecia) -- these pairings are pharmacologically accurate and non-contradictory but are not stated anywhere in the cited sources, so they are outside-knowledge additions layered onto an otherwise fully sourced fact pattern.

SRC-013 (Node 8, 'Questioning Only') states verbatim: 'Malaria and vCJD rely solely on donor history because no FDA-approved screening tests exist for donors.' This directly supports key A. RTTI list in SRC-013 explicitly includes Malaria (not vCJD), which correctly sinks distractor C. Regional Babesia NAT (line 37) and PI approved for platelets/plasma damaging nucleic acids (line 59) are both accurately used to build distractors B and E without overclaiming. SRC-037 Node 65 ('updates to donor deferral criteria for malaria, vCJD, and MSM, shifting toward individual risk assessment') and SRC-003 Node 30 travel/exposure deferral entries corroborate the history-based framing. No invented facts found. Minor scope note: the question centers on testing-vs-history methodology rather than deferral duration/criteria per se, but it is defensibly within the 'Deferral Criteria' node since it explains the mechanism by which malaria/vCJD donors are deferred (history alone, absent lab confirmation).

SRC-013 (Node 8, Common Board Traps: 'Syphilis units') states verbatim: 'A reactive nontreponemal test (RPR) can be "saved" if a treponemal-specific test is negative, allowing release if double-labeled' — this directly supports key A and the disposition described. Distractor rationales are accurate: Western blot is sourced as the HIV supplemental test (not syphilis, line 45); the 12-month deferral figure in distractor C is sourced elsewhere (SRC-003 Node 30) for unrelated high-risk sexual/incarceration behavior, correctly making it a misapplied distractor; Syphilis is explicitly listed as an RTTI in both SRC-013 and SRC-003, correctly sinking distractor E. One minor gap: the explanatory label 'biologic false-positive' is not verbatim source language (the source only says the result can be 'saved') — this is standard, uncontroversial BB/TM terminology for this exact serologic pattern, so it does not change the correct answer, but it is technically an added interpretive gloss beyond the literal source text. Also flagging low-severity blueprint-mismatch: the stem's substantive content is a testing-algorithm/unit-disposition question (RPR/TP-PA discordance, double-labeling) rather than donor deferral duration/criteria specifically, though it is topically adjacent within Donor Evaluation.

SRC-013 (Node 8, Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls) states 'Donors may test transiently HBsAg positive for up to 28 days after an HBV vaccination,' and Diagnostic Approach lists neutralization as the supplemental test for HBsAg — both directly support key A, and the 3-week vaccination timing in the stem falls within the sourced 28-day window. Distractor C is correctly refuted: SRC-013 states only HIV and HCV look-back/notification is mandated by federal law (21 CFR 610.46/47), consistent with the option rationale. Distractor E is correctly refuted: the sourced 3-calendar-day retrieval/quarantine requirement is specified only for reactive HIV, HCV, WNV, ZIKV, or T. cruzi tests — HBsAg/HBV is not in that list, so applying it to HBsAg is a legitimate misapplied-rule distractor. No invented facts found. Low-severity blueprint-mismatch note: like Q1, this question's substantive content is a testing/confirmatory-algorithm and look-back-rule question rather than donor deferral duration/criteria specifically, though again topically adjacent within Donor Evaluation (option B does touch permanent deferral).

Core mechanism is well grounded: SRC-023 states verbatim 'Citrate Toxicity: Presents as perioral paresthesias, tingling in the extremities, or shivering' and 'Hypocalcemia: Citrate chelates ionized calcium, necessitating monitoring and possible IV or oral replacement.' SRC-010 independently corroborates under 'Donor and Therapeutic Apheresis': 'Common adverse effects include citrate toxicity, presenting as hypocalcemia' - this cross-source confirmation is reassuring for applying the mechanism to a donor (not just a therapeutic-apheresis patient). Distractors B (ACE-I/bradykinin causes hypotension, not paresthesia, and no ACE-I/hypotension in stem), D (splenic platelet mobilization unrelated to neuro symptoms), and E (>15% TBV causes hypovolemic shock, contradicted by stable vitals) are all correctly excluded and each underlying fact is present in SRC-023. Minor issue: distractor C adds 'hepatic metabolism' - the source only says 'Metabolism of citrate to bicarbonate' without naming the liver specifically; physiologically reasonable but not literally sourced, a low-severity over-specification in a wrong-answer option. Also SRC-023's '15% Rule' is textually scoped to 'therapeutic procedures'/'the patient,' not explicitly donors, though it's used only as an excluded distractor here so impact is minimal. Single best answer A is clearly supported and independently verified.

Directly and precisely grounded in SRC-023's 'Source-Grounded Teaching Pearls': 'Splenic Mobilization: Platelet counts fall less than predicted after a donor procedure because platelets are mobilized from the spleen during the collection.' The stem's framing (post-procedure count higher than volume-based prediction) is the logical equivalent of 'counts fall less than predicted.' Distractors are well-differentiated and none invent an unsupported mechanism that could plausibly be confused as correct: B misapplies citrate/calcium chelation (a real fact, but tied to hypocalcemia/paresthesia, not platelet count elevation) to an unrelated lab finding; C misapplies the real alkalosis fact to platelet-count accuracy (unsupported linkage); D correctly identifies that the 66% fibrinogen-depletion figure applies to a 1.0-PV TPE exchange with albumin, not to platelet collection, and is honestly flagged as such in its own rationale; E invents a causal link between the 15% extracorporeal-volume rule and platelet-mass preservation that is not stated anywhere in the source. This is the strongest-grounded of the three questions.

The keyed fact is present verbatim in SRC-023 under 'Prevention': 'Severe Hypotension: ACE inhibitors should be held for 24 to 48 hours before apheresis to prevent accumulation of bradykinin.' Within the closed set of options, A is uniquely supported - B (prophylactic IV calcium) only addresses citrate/hypocalcemia and is never described as prophylactic or ACE-I-related; C (heparin substitution) is scoped in the source only to 'specific procedures like lipoprotein apheresis'; D contradicts the explicit Prevention bullet; E (permanent deferral) is unsupported/disproportionate. However, flagging a real scope concern: SRC-023 is titled 'Subdomain Node 6 - Therapeutic Apheresis Guidelines' and every other fact around this bullet (TPE, TTP, plasma-volume dosing) concerns patients undergoing therapeutic procedures, not volunteer community platelet donors. SRC-010 does group 'Donor and Therapeutic Apheresis' as one subdomain, which offers some support for cross-application, but none of the four sources explicitly states this ACE-inhibitor-hold rule as a routine action for asymptomatic community plateletpheresis donors, and SRC-003's dedicated donor-deferral/medication-deferral section (Subdomain Node 30) lists specific medication deferrals (Proscar/Propecia/Accutane, Avodart, Soriatane, Tegison) but does not mention ACE inhibitors at all. The question therefore extrapolates a therapeutic-apheresis-context prevention rule into a specific donor-management action without direct source confirmation that this is standard donor-collection practice, which is a meaningful blueprint/source-fit gap even though it does not create a better-supported alternative among the given options.

Core fact (10.5 mL/kg cap inclusive of samples; proportional blood+anticoagulant reduction for donors <110 lb) is verbatim in SRC-003 line 231. The stem's specific causal framing ('primary reason... to keep the ratio... correct') is a reasonable, near-inevitable inference from the source's own word 'proportionately' rather than an explicitly stated rationale sentence, so flagged at low severity only. All three distractors (B: weight-based Hgb threshold - does not exist in source, thresholds are sex-based; C: donation interval - unrelated fixed 8-week rule; D: TTI testing - unrelated to anticoagulant ratio) are correctly identified as unsupported/wrong. Single best answer, no ambiguity. Minor blueprint fit note: this content (blood/anticoagulant volume during the draw) reasonably belongs under 'Whole Blood' collection process.

All values exactly match SRC-003: female Hb >=12.5 g/dL (Hct >=38%), male Hb >=13.0 g/dL (Hct >=39%), autologous Hb >=11.0 g/dL. Donor's 12.6 g/dL correctly exceeds the female threshold; distractors B (wrong sex threshold), C (invented 40% Hct figure, unsourced), and D (conflates autologous/male thresholds) are all correctly identified as wrong per source. Single best answer, fully grounded. Blueprint-mismatch flag: cited node is 'Collection Process > Blood Collection > Whole Blood,' but this question tests donor eligibility/screening criteria (a physical-exam prerequisite) rather than the whole blood collection process itself; content sits under SRC-003's donor-selection/physical-exam subsection, not a collection-procedure subsection.

Donation interval of 8 weeks (56 days) for whole blood is stated verbatim in SRC-003 line 233. 42 days < 56 days, so deferral is correctly required despite meeting all other physical criteria. Distractors B (physical criteria override interval), C (autologous relabeling, unsupported and irrelevant), and D (permanent deferral, contradicted by source's distinct permanent-deferral list for HBsAg-positive/CJD, not interval violations) are all correctly wrong per source. Single best answer, fully grounded. Same blueprint-mismatch note as Q1: this tests donor interval eligibility rather than the whole blood collection process itself.

SRC-032 (line 159) states 'FFP must be separated and frozen within 8 hours (or 6 hours if in ACD); PF24 within 24 hours' and (line 163) 'Thawed Plasma...is deficient in labile Factors V and VIII' as two separate, unconnected bullet points in the same subsection. The stem's core ask -- the 'underlying reason' FFP has a stricter freeze window than PF24 -- is never stated explicitly as a causal link in any of the 4 approved sources; the item synthesizes it by juxtaposing two adjacent facts. The synthesis is standard, correct transfusion-medicine physiology (confirmed in a different, non-approved source: essentials-of-plasma-and-platelet-transfusion-medicine.md, which explicitly says PF24 'contains slightly lower levels of labile factors (V and VIII)... compared to FFP'), so option B is still clearly the best/only defensible answer among the five and is not contradicted by anything in the approved set. But per strict grounding rules, the causal mechanism itself is inferred rather than sourced, so this is 'partial' not 'pass'. Also flagging blueprint-mismatch: source organizes this content under 'Subdomain Node 19 – Blood Component Manufacturing & QC,' not under a 'Collection Process > Component Separation' framing, so the node mapping is debatable though not unreasonable. All other option rationales (A bacterial, C leukoreduction, D fibrinogen, E FVIII-only) are correctly refuted by/consistent with the source.

Directly and precisely grounded: SRC-032 states WBD platelets must contain >=5.5x10^10 platelets with pH >=6.2 at outdate, and apheresis platelets require >=3.0x10^11 with the same pH minimum (lines 151, 153). The scenario (4.8x10^10 platelets, pH 6.5) unambiguously passes pH and fails platelet content, matching key A exactly. All distractor rationales (B both-pass, C pH-fail, D wrong-spec-apheresis, E no-minimum-exists) are correctly and specifically refuted by the same two source lines. No invented facts. Minor blueprint-mismatch flag only: this is QC/spec content the source itself titles 'Blood Component Manufacturing & QC,' arguably once-removed from the 'Collection > Component Separation' node label, though defensible as a natural extension of separating/preparing the platelet component.

Core rule is directly and correctly sourced: SRC-032 line 171 states a 300-404 mL collection into a 450-mL bag must be labeled 'low volume' and no FFP can be made from it; 340 mL falls squarely in that range, making A the clear key and B/C/D/E correctly refuted (no discard, no full FFP processing, no apheresis relabeling loophole, no indefinite quarantine described anywhere in source). However, the explanation_correct and teaching_point both add a specific causal mechanism -- 'the anticoagulant-to-blood ratio was designed for the full collection volume' -- that does not appear anywhere in any of the 4 approved sources; this is a plausible/standard rationale but is an unsourced addition layered onto an otherwise well-grounded item. Also carries the same mild blueprint-mismatch concern as the other two items in this set (source frames this as 'Common Board Traps' under Component Manufacturing & QC rather than under a Collection/Component-Separation process node).

Core factual ingredients are directly grounded: SRC-026 states prestorage LR to <5x10^6 WBCs/unit 'significantly reduces the risk of primary HLA alloimmunization' and explicitly flags 'Platelets do NOT express HLA Class II antigens. Antibodies to Class II indicate sensitization to contaminating leukocytes in the product.' SRC-028 confirms Class I is expressed on 'most nucleated cells and platelets' while Class II expression 'is restricted to antigen-presenting cells (APCs), such as B lymphocytes and macrophages,' and that Class III encodes complement/cytokines (supports distractor C). SRC-026 also confirms contaminating RBCs (not leukocytes) cause RhD sensitization, a distinct phenomenon (supports distractor D's rationale). All five option rationales trace to these facts and no option contradicts the sources. However, the specific causal/mechanistic framing in the keyed answer and its rationale -- that contaminating donor leukocytes 'serve as antigen-presenting cells' that actively present alloantigen to drive primary sensitization -- is a synthesis across two sources (SRC-026 + SRC-028) rather than a single explicit statement of that causal chain in either source; the sources state the two supporting facts separately (leukocytes are the origin of Class II antibodies; APCs express Class II) but do not spell out the antigen-presentation mechanism in these words. This is a reasonable, standard transfusion-medicine inference and not contradicted, but strict grounding calls for 'partial' rather than 'pass' on that inferential leap. No other defensible answer exists given the source's explicit 'HLA Class II Distractor' framing, which maps directly onto this stem -- A is the clear single best answer with no credible competing choice among B-E, each of which is either factually wrong (B: LR does not strip antigens from platelet membranes; platelets retain Class I) or addresses an unrelated mechanism (C: MHC III/complement, D: RBC/RhD, E: platelet dose) per the sources.

Core claim is well grounded: SRC-032 (Node 16, Definitions) defines dilutional coagulopathy verbatim as 'a hemorrhagic state caused by replacing lost whole blood with only RBCs and crystalloids, leading to depleted factor and platelet levels,' and the same node states balanced 1:1:1 resuscitation prevents this — matching the stem's 8u RBC + 6L crystalloid with zero plasma/platelets. The normothermia detail correctly eliminates hypothermia (SRC-032: 'Hypothermia... exacerbate coagulopathy by reducing enzymatic activity'), and 'no plasma or platelets given' correctly eliminates citrate toxicity (SRC-032: 'plasma/platelets contain 5x more citrate than RBCs'). Vitamin K deficiency (PIVKA) and DIC (concurrent procoagulant/fibrinolytic activation) are both sourced from SRC-002 Node 47. Minor gap: option B's rationale lists specific DIC triggers ('no sepsis, malignancy, or amniotic fluid embolism trigger') that are not stated anywhere in the four approved sources — this is outside-source clinical knowledge added by the writer, though it does not change the correct answer. Independently, A is the best-supported answer given the sources' explicit definition.

All three sourced MTP triggers are quoted almost verbatim from SRC-032 Node 16: platelets <50,000/µL, plasma for INR>1.5 or aPTT>60s, cryoprecipitate for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL — confirmed again in SRC-043's Domain 2 map ('Cryo for fibrinogen <100 mg/dL'). Patient values (platelets 78k, INR 1.3, aPTT 38s, fibrinogen 85) cleanly cross only the fibrinogen threshold, making cryoprecipitate the single defensible next step. Ionized calcium 1.1 is above the sourced >1.0 mmol/L target so no calcium-correction option competes. PCC is correctly excluded since the sources tie it only to warfarin reversal, not MTP hypofibrinogenemia. No unsupported claims found; independently verified A is correct.

The key fact is directly and clearly sourced: SRC-002 Node 47 states verbatim 'Urgent Warfarin Reversal: PCC is preferred over FFP due to its lower volume and faster normalization of the INR,' and the same node ties rFVIIa/PCC bypassing agents specifically to high-titer Factor VIII inhibitors, correctly excluding option D. Cryoprecipitate is correctly excluded as it targets fibrinogen deficiency (sourced separately), not VKA effect. However, option C's rationale asserts vitamin K 'has a delayed onset of action... does not provide rapid factor replacement' — this specific claim (onset timing) is not stated anywhere in the four approved sources; the sources only mention vitamin K in the unrelated PIVKA-deficiency context (SRC-002 Node 47). This is true clinical knowledge but not source-grounded per the strict audit standard, so support is partial rather than full pass. Independently, A remains the best answer.

SRC-032 (Subdomain Node 16 - MTP, Clinical Presentations) states verbatim: 'Hypothermia: Transfusion of refrigerated components can cause cardiac arrhythmias and exacerbate coagulopathy by reducing enzymatic activity.' This directly supports keyed answer B. Distractor A correctly isolates citrate toxicity (separately described in the source as causing hypocalcemia via anticoagulant citrate, not via hypothermia) as a distinct mechanism. Distractor C correctly reflects the source's separate definition of dilutional coagulopathy (replacement with RBCs/crystalloid alone) - the stem even signals this is not the answer by stating transfusion occurred 'in a balanced ratio.' Distractor D correctly reflects the source's separate hyperkalemia/arrhythmia mechanism (RBC storage potassium leak), not a coagulopathy mechanism. All four option rationales are grounded and mutually exclusive; no ambiguity in the keyed answer.

SRC-032 states lab triggers verbatim: platelets if <50,000/uL, plasma if INR >1.5 or aPTT >60 sec, cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <100 mg/dL, and ionized calcium goal >1.0 mmol/L.
